I was super excited about this but the paper started coming apart almost immediately on several of the pieces. I bought 2 puzzles and this was the case with both. Also, only 1 of the pictures is magnetic so for younger kids who want to build the puzzle on top of the pictures instead of next to it they can only do that with one of them.
Love this puzzle! It was a lot of fun to do because of the subtleties of color in Lewitt’s work.
My wife loved it. Very good quality.

I really enjoyed this puzzle. The colors were very pretty and vibrant. After I finished, I was able to pick the puzzle up and flip it over to see the image on the other side.  That’s how well the pieces held together.  However...I must say that I was not as impressed with the image on the back. Let’s just say that I will only be putting this puzzle together with the color side up.  This was still a good buy for the price.  I will buy more puzzles from this company.

I was a little intimidated to order a double-sided puzzle, but this is awesome! One side is glossy and the other is matte, and it's very easy to distinguish between the two. The colors differentiate just enough to be challenging but not impossible.

I'll admit that while working on the black and white side, I'd flip a piece over to see about where it fit on the color side as a small cheat. I was able to do both sides on a lazy Saturday, enough to occupy your mind without pulling your hair out!

I would love to pass it on, but I will definitely be doing this puzzle again before I do!
Para regalar
Working on this puzzle with my girls and it truly is a beautiful puzzle.  We were surprised at how much better we liked it in person, the gold is really beautiful.

I have been doing a lot of puzzles, specially now with the pandemic. I love this one because of the great quality of the print and also the thickness of the pieces. I love there is a print of the final product so two people can work at the same time. With 1K puzzle, an extra print is priceless.
Best made, most creative and fabulous puzzles!
I love the puzzles created by this manufacture. They are challenging yet enjoyable with oddly shaped pieces that add to the challenge!

It was indeed delightful - a family member and I worked on it during stormy times being shut up in the house.

I have ordered several Galison puzzles to see us through the winter. When people would stop by, they would take some time to work on it too.

My 94 year old mother always has a 500 piece puzzle out she's working on.  She loves Galison puzzles  - they have a lot of choices in the 500 piece range.  They also provide a puzzle picture in the box and, even it isn't very large, it allows 2 people to each have an image they can look at close up.  The price of the 500 piece puzzles is very fair and their quality is consistent.  The artist's they feature have colorful and fun images.

Galison - Greenhouse Gardens - 500 Pieces - I didn’t realise it was random cut until I opened it up. Grey cardboard & lots of puzzle dust. Very unlike other Galison 500 piece puzzles I have done. I didn’t enjoy it to begin with as it wasn’t the hard, crisp pieces of two different shapes that I was used to from previous Galison 500 piece puzzles. By the end I liked it and the finished item is lovely but I didn’t enjoy the process as much as I have enjoyed other puzzles. Lovely image however.

Beautiful puzzle. Been here many times growing so caught my attention. Nicely cut, sharp colors, no dust, extra picture page for help.
Not for a beginner .
only starting this puzzle but the entire bottom side is not depicted anywhere…not sure about other sides yet

I love Galison puzzles, but this one is a total fail. It's a nice-looking puzzle and the several extra-large pieces are a fun idea, but like another reviewer said, the large pieces were all slightly warped and didn't lay flat. When finished, it looked terrible. Galison needs to either figure out how to keep large pieces of cut carboard totally flat or redesign so that all of the pieces are a somewhat uniform size. Very unsatisfied.
This is a high quality puzzle (as all products from this company).  The artwork is cute. I appreciate a puzzle that represents people of color.

As a fan of Charley Harper's work, I really love these Pomegranate Puzzles! I really enjoy this brand of puzzle, for the pieces are crisply cut and the puzzles have a nice finish. This image in particular is quite nice. For serious puzzle people, it will be easy to put together. Nonetheless, it makes for a very fun project!
Beautiful puzzle. Interesting challenge. Not frustratingly difficult, but not too simple. Clever image.

Great fun but challenging. Enjoyed the unusual subject matter. Love
all of Charley Harpers work. Have most of the puzzles and plan to
purchase others.
Great puzzle,  Happy recipient.

I enjoy wildlife and birds... the the Charley Harper wildlife renditions are phenomenal and challenging... I truly enjoyable jigsaw puzzles and this puzzle is his best.  But then I say that about all Charley Harper art.
It is so cute and pretty to look at. It is not that difficult to complete it but fun to work on. Great quality.

The picture was originally one of a series that Harper did for the US Park Service.  Most of these posters have sold out.  The artist has become very popular among certain groups of people.  This puzzle is now mounted, in a frame on my wall.  Buying the puzzle was the most affordable way to get the picture.
Very happy with puzzle. Shipping was fast. Fun puzzles have been hard to find during the pandemic and I appreciate that it came quickly and in great condition!
Well-made and a pleasure to assemble. An extended way to experience the great work of this graphic artist!
The print is clear and colorful. The pieces are sturdy and thick, holding together relatively tight. The surface is matte so looks high quality but it feels a pity if you glue it together.
Loved the puzzle.  It came quickly and was exactly as described and pictured. I'd recommend this puzzle to anyone looking to pass the time on a rainy day.  It would look great framed as well.

This puzzle offers the perfect balance between challenging your puzzling skills and not driving you to give up. This painting of
Charley Harper's adds frustrating fun because much of the image area is a reflection of the mountains surrounding a beaver pond...something extra to challenge the brain. Before we were done, our puzzle-shunning 14 year old son joined in, and suggested we get another Charley Harper puzzle after we finished.

This is a beautiful puzzle and a chance to enjoy Charley Harper's whimsical scene.  This puzzle is both challenging and enjoyable. The colors are vivid and the pieces are sturdy!  I definitely recommend!
Many pieces has light fixing on them which in the dark areas is distracting.  I'm a Charlie Harper fan so I'll buy the images I like but wasn't overly impressed with quality for the price.

The box looks beautiful but unfortunately 3 pieces are missing, they are consecutive pieces from one of the corner. Must be an error at the production when they cut the picture....  so my puzzles sadly never be completed...
Awesome. Loved it in person!
Excellent product - I ordered as a gift as the recipient loves these puzzles. It shipped quickly and arrived well ahead of time.
Excellent quality, buying from same manufacturer again

Love Charlie Harper puzzles.  My go to when I need an "easier" puzzle.  Just enough challenge to keep it interesting. The art is gorgeous and there are so many hidden things within the picture.  And I think Pomegranent (sp?) is one of the best brands with high quality pieces.
We like the Charley Harper paintings, and Pomegranate is a quality puzzle maker.

Raise your puzzle game by tackling this delightful Charley Harper puzzle. You'll find yourself checking bird guides and flower guides as you ponder each section of the Sierra Nevada puzzle.  Suitable for intermediate puzzlers who want a challenge and masters who want to practice a timed drill. Can be done in under three hours.

Challenging, high quality puzzle. The artwork makes for a perfect poster sized puzzle and I glued and framed it once I was done. Looking forward to being a repeat purchaser of Charley Harper Pomegranate puzzles.

I have never had an issue with a Pomegranate made puzzle before but this one was horrible which was a shame. Beautiful image, well printed matte finish so easy on the eyes, but the pieces were cut horribly with many many arriving with bits cut off because they were too thin to remain attached. Also missing an edge piece. The puzzle was new and sealed so not sure why it was a mess... maybe a second that slipped through or something. I am returning it. Again this is the first pomegranate puzzle I have found to be not well made.

Right out of the sealed plastic packet, everyone locates the edge pieces first. When I could only find three corner pieces, I became concerned. Also, a very small piece fell out of the bag. As it turns out, the small piece was actually cut off of a larger puzzle tile. The knobs are too close together and the puzzle design is inherently weak. The fourth corner was, indeed, missing - along with four other pieces all together in one corner. I love this artist and the puzzle pieces fit together nicely but these are problems that should be avoided if the puzzle is to be acceptable.

FOLLOW UP: The publisher has been very good in responding to my issue and has replaced the puzzle. It may be a little while before I can do the puzzle to see if the replacement is better than the original but I am pleased that the publisher seems to care about customer service. Thanks!

Awesome!  Make sure you have a really good full spectrum lamp for this one, because the dark brown, black, and navy are very close. The matte finish is high quality, as are the thick pieces. I glued and mounted this on black poster board and it looks great!  Just challenging enough to be fun.

I rated this 5 stars because I love the Order of the Stick books....but the main reason I'm posting this is the INSANE price...go to ookoodook dot com....it's only $29.95 NEW, not $120 from this ripoff seller!

Volume two of the Order of the Stick series has great art, dialouge, and storytelling. It's very clever, and doesn't fall into so many of the ruts that many webcomics or gaming cartoons fall prey to. At nearly 250 pages, it's worth the money, and that's before you consider the additional material in the book that's not available on the website.

Don't click on the New Paperback version, as you will be taken to Dungeon Crawlin Fools, rather than No Cure for the Paladin Blues.  And if you're like me, and you don't notice, you will order the wrong product which you will then wrap up and give to your husband on Christmas Day.  And he will look at you and wonder how you went from hanging on to his every word to not listening to him at all in 10 short years, and he will sigh gently.

Been writing a lot lately so I thought this could help a bit when I needed to generate something different for my dungeons.  It's okay, but many of the tables are pretty short and frankly, most of the ideas aren't that different or interesting.  You'll actually find better tables in the first edition DMG or any number of other OSR RPG guides.  So unless you're really starved for mediocre ideas, save your $20 or invest in something with more meat to it.

Judge Dress the cursed Earth is a reimplemented version of The Lost Expedition, owning both games I seem to play Judge Dress more. The artwork is amazing, it's the same style of the graphic novel. If you have played the Lost Expedition then you already know how to play this game, it follows the same structure. Now I have only played both games as a solo game, so I can only attest to the solo mode, during the dawn phase you draw 6 cards from the encounter deck, then draw two more from the encounter deck face up on the table, then put them in numerical order, then play two more cards from your hand, then draw one more from the deck, then play one last card from your hand, that's the encounters you are going to have for the dawn phase. In the pictures you will see some new icons, such as Radiation. Radiation works like this, Everytime you encounter radiation you place a radiation chit on a character card, if ever the number of radiation chits is more then the number of health,that character is dead. So you have to balance that out between all characters. Then each character has there own specialties, Dredd has survival, Judge Giant has diplomacy, and Judge Anderson has Psychic powers. The goal of the game is to beat the perps to a specific location card, and have at least one living judge. The components are good quality, the chits are small, but they work. The chit box is great, it looks like a field ration kit, the card quality is great, big cards, bright artwork, and everything is very thematic to the source material. If you want to see a solo playthrough, One stop co-op shop has one on YouTube. If you enjoyed the Lost Expedition game, then you'll enjoy this.

This system is everything I've ever wanted out of a TTRPG. No roll is ever boring, as everything has the potential for consequences arising. There's no such thing as "We all want to make perception checks" for 20 minutes in this system, and the pace is greatly elevated as a result. In fact, that example sticks closely to the entire core concept for Blades in the Dark, which is "Cut to the action."
The game supports you along this journey with an interesting and fleshed out city to explore. One city may sound small, but for your players (a lowly gang of criminals) it becomes a true playground. Balancing between trying not to get caught but getting away with the most coin possible in each session becomes a tightrope act, and not every character is going to make it. The city is lethal, and even those who survive may find themselves taking on permanent changes to their character as trauma builds up.
As doom and gloom as it sounds, the game in action is actually a blast of energy that easily allows a group of players to all have their moment in the spotlight in a single session. Because the system doesn't get bogged down in numbers bloat, the dice are easily readable at any level, and the flow of the game doesn't get broken by everyone having to crack open the corebook again to understand the rules. Once Blades clicks (and it does so fairly quickly), it's hard to go back to other TTRPG's.
If you're new to the hobby, this is honestly the best start you could have. If you've been playing for years, you owe it to yourself to see the ideas on display here. Buy a copy just to read, and maybe you'll get just as excited as I did about running a game yourself.

I shrugged at Blades in the Dark when it first came out because at the time I wasn't fond of games that had built-in settings. A friend of mine picked it up which put it on my radar. I decided to give it a go and I think it's probably the greatest system I've ever read. I tried reading through Apocalypse World and Dungeon World, but I felt like they were missing...something. Blades managed to fill that void. It brought in all of the right tools, and for the first time, I see the beauty of fiction-first games.

First and foremost, literally *everything* is on theme. The art evokes the setting wonderfully because (I think) it's all done by the game's creator. The font choice and page layout are so clear and concise that I feel as if it's meant to represent the clear-cut streets of Doskvol (the game's setting). The writing feels like the game's creator has taken you under his wing and is trying to train you to become a master. After each new subsystem is introduced, the author stops for a moment and gives you questions to ask yourself. He's not just teaching you his game, he's teaching you how to teach yourself.

Next, the game's setting was built alongside the mechanics, so everything the setting has to offer is useable and exists for a clear purpose. There are factions to befriend/anger, landmarks to get more familiar with the city, and plenty of places to score. The window dressing such as the fact that the city is haunted by spirits and that the sun went out a thousand years ago are welcome additions as well, as they make the game world feel all the grittier.

Finally, the mechanics have a symbiotic relationship with the game's overall tone and themes. It's a game about heists in a dark world, and there are ticking clocks, nonstop action, and complications galore. Every roll the players make can very easily cascade into more complications and problems which makes the world feel more real and successful scores feel more earned.

If you love tabletop RPGs, buy this book because it will undoubtedly inspire the next generation of game designers. Buy it, read it, play it, hack it, and see where it takes you. If you read it and don't like it, you might need to just read/play more games and up your taste. I was (and still am) a hardcore old-school gamer and I never thought a narrative game would sway me over to the genre. Blades in the Dark is a masterpiece so teeming with flavor that it makes all of those settingless catch-all games feel less complete.

The idea of a book of improv exercises didn't immediately appeal to me because it can be hard to get a group together willing to try something so different from our usual games. I decided to give this book a chance anyway after I kept hearing glowing reviews from people I respect. I'm really glad I did.

The book manages to accomplish a lot of things at once:

** Collection of interactive exercises for groups. It provides numerous improv exercises from warm-ups to exercises that focus on different skills (e.g. ways to communicate character through posture and speech; how to create a character out of 1 starting concept; how to play off others, etc.). The exercises are a great foundation in improv skills, but can also be used for learning basic performance skills, group ice-breakers, and some are just entertaining games. I could see some of these exercises as a way to get my stand-up comedian friends to move towards roleplaying games.

** Solid introduction to improv. While the book applies its lessons to help players interested in LARPs and tabletop roleplaying, the content also acts as a great improv foundation. It introduces important principles, then applies them to gaming examples. I found this mix of theory and application made everything easier to understand and remember.

** Gaming-specific application for improv skills. So the book provides great exercises and teaching, but how does that relate to gaming? As it turns out, really well. Even if you can't find people to do the exercises, the text is filled with practical applications. It takes the often quoted "Yes, and..." and applies it to common roleplaying dice mechanics (e.g. a full success is "yes, and..." while a partial or mixed success a la Apocalypse World is "yes, but . . ."). The chapter on creating characters by starting with one facet (e.g. this person taps their foot) and then asking, "If that, then what?" (e.g. maybe they are always in a hurry) gave me a new way to think about building NPCs for the games I run.

** Tips on relationship dynamics in roleplay. Since improv often involves multiple people playing off each other, participants have a big impact on each others' performance. The same is true in gaming, but I never thought about the details of why and what that means until this book. For one example, the book explains why argument scenes don't work well in improv: it's fun for the person saying "No," but not for the person put on the defensive. This made me rethink how I play out NPC resistance as a GM. The book has an entire section on the idea of "status" in relationship dynamics, and how you can use status shifting to keep arguments interesting. (And also highlights how mixing a character's status and showing strength and vulnerability keeps them interesting).

I started reading Improv for Gamers thinking it wasn't for me, but came away really excited by its insights, with a good toolbox of group exercises, and a new way of thinking about roleplay. Highly recommended!

I would call this the best tool I have for those that want to improve their gaming experience.  The book is full of fun exercises which teach you skills you can apply immediately at the table or in a LARP.  I now feel more confident thinking on my feet when presented with new situations in games.  I also appreciate the lessons that improve listening skills.  I have noticed the impact of this not only in game but in life.  I can't recommend this book enough.

So, friends. I decided to have my FIRST EVIL PC! Super exciting. Not only that, but I made her the witchiest warlock ever. Super high charisma. Super high deception. Super selfish. I envisioned her as a conniving and clever, wheelin' and dealin', double-talking creepy old lady. And then at the table, what happened? I spoke in my regular, bubbly voice, stayed silent during any social interactions (like I usually do), and didn't take any opportunities to be shady or weird. If an NPC engaged with me, I would just give the most simple, honest response. Bummer.

So I got Improv for Gamers, hoping to learn some tricks to being on the spot at the table. And I have to say, if you are a shy gamer or have a hard time staying in character bcs you feel insecure, you will likely benefit from this book. Conversely, I would say if you are a super confident gamer and people accuse you of messing up the plot by taking things off the rails too much, you might benefit from this book as well! And if you are a GM, no matter what your style, I think you would benefit from this book.

This book is much more improv-focused than RPG-focused, and I will say some of the "gaming" discussion seems a wee bit tacked on. But I prefer possibly tacked-on gaming perspective vs an improv book with no gaming perspective at all because then, well, it would basically be just an improv book, eh?

I also wish this book had more advice for people who don't have a roomful of friends. There have to be some exercises improvisers do when they're on their own. Unless your group knows you bought it, it's going to seem weird to show up on game day and be like, "Hey ya'll, let's throw an invisible sound-effect ball around for a while!" In fact, I've only done exercises so far with my partner, and there is only so much you can do in a two-person scene.

That being said, I SO appreciated the gentle, kind nature of this book. Just like a good RPG, the emphasis is on teamwork, relationships, and moving the story along in a meaningful way. Recommended!

This is a must buy for anyone who enjoyed the original release of Civilization from Fantasy Flight. The added pieces for an additional player are nice but the new investment system finally makes the coins relevant to the game, so that even if you're not going for an economic victory you can get some serious benefits. Additionally, the new great people deck makes gaining great people more interesting, and certainly easier as you are now drawing from a deck to determine which great person you get as opposed to sifting through a pile of tokens. After several plays the only drawback I see is that some of the new civilizations are a bit overpowered for two player games, but that is a minor inconvenience compared to all the other content.

This is a great expansion that not only adds to the game, but balances it out.

I found playing Civilization that it was possible to win every way except by building a tech pyramid. It just took too long and by the time you built a level 4 tech, someone was about to win through another means. This expansion adds ways of making tech victories possible.

There are also additional Civilizations, Wonders, Techs, and more. The addition of being able to play with a fifth player is another perk.

If you thoroughly enjoy the Civilization board game you will not regret buying this expansion as well.

Amazing product. It arrived on the day I run a weekly game. The first portion of our session ended up involving some time in an office setting that I had not planned for. I am normally somewhat incredulous of published homebrew content for Dungeons and Dragons or similar game systems - people often enough have great conceptual ideas that they lack the experience or knowledge to stat out and balance properly. Among those I trust near implicitly to do this task well (besides the talented folks at Middle Finger of Vecna) are the publishers of the Tome of Beasts; Kobold Press.

The stat blocks in the Tome of Beasts are well made and easy to read, and while they lack the aesthetic flair of official Dungeons and Dragons bestiaries, they still possess beautiful and striking artwork on nearly every page that do great work bringing the creatures described to life, alongside a minimum of one paragraph and often more of lore that can be applied to a variety of settings should one wish to leave it largely unmodified - and of course every creature inside fits neatly into Kobold Press' homebrew setting Midgard. (Even going so far as to include additional sidebars about where certain creatures fit into the Midgard setting geographically and historically)

All in all, even if you don't run a campaign in Midgard, if you're a DM who has a well used copy of the Monster Manual, Volo's Guide to Monsters, and Modenkainen's Tome of Foes on your shelf, the Tome of Beasts is a wonderful addition to your bestiary collection.

While softcovered and smaller than the full-size print version, the 'Pocket Edition' is of great quality especially for the price, shouldn't prove difficult to read for most people, though if you have problems with eyesight or small text, the full-size version may be worth the investment.

I've had this book for exactly 24 hours now, I already have 3 encounters planned from this book. There's a lot of creatures in here with some being brand new and some being variants of existing creatures. Lots of good stuff to spice up your campaign.

You'll probably see comments about the book being delivered with the cover ripped off or damaged. While mine arrived in good condition, I can definitely tell you this cover feels like it's made to rip off and I'm not even remotely surprised that people are having that problem. But don't let that scare you off, it's a very good book.

There are some things about it that are a little unexpected. First, I'm not that familiar with the Midgard world setting from Kobold press, and there are a number of references to it. I've been able to dig around online and find any info I was wanting, so it's not that big of a deal. There are some "monsters" that like in D&D publications are stated out versions of NPC races. That's super handy. What isn't handy is the lack of the base stat package. For example, there are 2 different minotaur types mentioned, but no base one for Player characters or template for generic Minotaur NPCs. Their monsters are generally well designed and thought out. There are even rules for a "Monster" called Bar Brawl. It's an interesting take on what can be an insanely hard thing to role play.

Overall, a well done, beautiful hard cover book.

The Tome of Beasts is a great 3rd party monster menagerie for 5th edition Dungeons & Dragons that justifies its price tag by being 400+ page long and absolutely packed with content. Besides including a lot of totally original monsters, it also includes a handful Lovecraftian monsters (like the Shoggoth) and obscure mythological creatures like Koschei the Deathless and the Egyptian "serpopard". The monster descriptions also hint at a lot of cool elements from the Kobold Press "Midgard" campaign setting, and I personally find this sort of lore useful when brainstorming ideas for my own games. It also feels the the ToB was designed to include types of monsters that weren't represented well in the official Monster Manual, as it includes several lower CR constructs and what feels like a high proportion of monsters with the fey type. For DMs running higher level campaigns, the book includes a number of unique demons,devils, and fey as well as some CR 20+ dragons and other "boss" style monsters which look likely to provide memorable, challenging battles.

Physically, the book is well put together and feels like it has been built to last. There are full color illustrations for every monster, although it seems like there were text tweaks to some monsters after the art was finalized. (For example, the Dream-Eater is depicted as a sword-wielding humanoid but it's stats reflect claw and bite attacks.) There are also a few unclear bits of text in some of the monster mechanics, but the publisher, Kobold Press, has an errata document available at their website. Overall the production values here seem high and the presentation is attractive.

That said, I have two gameplay concerns from regarding the Tome of Beasts, and depending on your style of play they may or may not impact your enjoyment of the product. The first is that some of the monsters have fiddly mechanics that I find cumbersome, but this is something that a DM can easily modify or ignore. The second is that the challenge ratings for a lot of the monsters seem significantly lower than they ought to be. This is especially true of some of the lower CR monsters. Again, as a DM you can circumvent this by either tweaking the monsters or assuming a higher challenge rating, but it can be a pain if you're using this resource in conjunction an encounter-builder like Kobold Fight Club. I would, however, strongly recommend that any DM whose party runs a Circle of the Moon Druid have a look at the beasts presented in this book before allowing it at your table. There are several CR 1 monsters with the beast type that are a bit unbalanced in the hands of a 2nd level character, in particular the dogmole, which averages 71 hit points and has attacks which can deal 28 points of damage in a turn.

Overall I've been very happy with the Tome of Beasts. In terms of the ratio of content to cost, this book is a steal. Just keep in mind as a DM that you'll probably have to do some tweaking.
Most of the book is fantastic! Unfortunately some of the pages are double printed on some the pages making it slightly hard to read a few of them but other than that it’s a great book!

This book is absolutely _packed_ with incredible monsters. As far as the stats are concerned, they seem pretty well balanced compared to first party material, so I'm pretty comfortable running these in my campaigns at the listed CR. The big thing though is that almost every single one of these monsters has some kind of "interesting" ability. Even things that are "extensions" of the core monsters, like the new gnolls or the small handful of new dinosaurs all have a cool 1/day, recharge 5-6 ability or special trait. It's definitely inspiring that if the CR wasn't quite right for a monster I really liked the flavor of, I could strip those cool abilities off and put them on a generic creature with the right CR from the Monster Manual and have the neat encounter the Tome of Beasts wants you to have.

Also, while there are definitely a few new Ancient Dragons, Fey Lords and BBEG statblocks, the majority of the monsters are already concentrated nicely between CR 4 to 8 or so, which is certainly where most of my campaigns spend most of their time.

There's a good variety of monsters, but there are also a notably large number of desert/sand creatures and lots of kinds of good and evil fey. The desert creatures in particular feel like they make a desert-based campaign/arc possible to a degree that I think oceanic/underwater campaigns have always been, which I didn't know I wanted, but am totally inspired by now.

Compared to the first party monster supplements, this is better by an order of magnitude. For Mordenkainen's and Volo's, Wizards decided to focus so much of the book on monster lore and culture but I don't know who really needs that. If you're running modules, then that lore is kind of baked in for you, and if you're homebrewing and your campaign involves, say, mind flayers, then you've probably already figured out how they fit into your world. If you liked the Monster Manual and just want more of that, then skip MToF/VGtM and buy this!

I do have a few criticisms, though none that prevent this from being the best 5E monster supplement on the market:
1. There are no indices by CR/type/environment. The book is already huge, so they probably got cut for space, but on the flip side the enormous book means you're going to need one. Fortunately, there are several available online
2. There's a lot of sirens/nymphs/dryads/sexy lady who lures stupid men to their deaths. Some of them are legitimate folklore classics that weren't in the MM (not an accident on WotC's part, I suspect), but they're all basically the same and just play into this terrible unwelcoming trope that we ought to be fighting, not supporting. It's weird.
Great little game, enough randomness to keep in spirit of the theme...enough strategy to mess with other players plans.  Game quality is fantastic

This game is alot of fun. Me and my husband enjoy playing board games and this is one of our favorites! It is not hard to learn (I don't like playing games where all you do is read the rule book more than you actually play the game). The games are not super long which is perfect for us because we get bored very easy with long games. With this game we can get about 3 games in before dinner (loser cooks...wonder what he is making tonight?)

Bought this game because I liked the board style and the artwork, and didn't really know what I was getting into. I have to admit I am pleased with this game, but it only gets 3 stars because it could have been so much better. The characters, abilities, boards, and goals are all fantastic. Generally these goals involve you defeating an opponent or boss in battle (a dice roll plus character and item modifiers), or they may require you to bring an item to a specific place on the map. A typical game game lasts about half an hour, and its pretty fair between the characters.

However where this game lacks is the event cards. These cards have little to nothing to do with Wonderland aside from the artwork on the cards. Some of these require you roll a pair of dice within a time limit, while others require you answer a question asked by another player. Some trivia would be fine...however these cards literally just say "Have another player ask you a question. If you get it right you succeed this event" Such open ended cards like this really tear down the 4th wall and leave this game lacking.

If you're looking for a fairly simple game that many people can play I do recommend this game. I have gotten about 5-6 plays in since I bought this game, but you may wish to adapt or remove a number of these event cards like we have done.

This game is a beast! So much cardboard and plastic bits.. still in the process of painting miniatures and loving it. The only bad thing was that the corner of my box was split.. so heads up if you're a collector.

This game is inspired by Games Workshop's 1995 dungeon crawler Warhammer Quest, and as such it has a similar level of awesomesauce and almost as much crazy randomness. Your experience and mileage will vary depending on how many of the numerous expansions you end up buying. The setting for this specific game is the late 19th century Old West, but there are portals to other times and places which means the characters could (eventually) end up in Feudal Japan, a dying alien planet, a lava cave, medieval Viking territory, a Lovecraftian nightmare beast's entrails, etc. etc.

This game debuted in 2014 but I didn't play it until a few years later. Then I bought the Forbidden Fortress game so that I could have a different setting than my siblings. It sat on my shelf untouched for a year or so. Then I pulled it out, assembled, and actually played the game. Huge fun was had, and now I find myself collecting the backlog of related products like a fiend and developing a meta-system to determine which worlds the players may end up in. The advantage to the lore of this game is that there is nothing stopping your team of cowboys from stepping into a portal and ending up in another dungeon-crawler game setting altogether, for example the Warhammer Old World or Cursed City! Whereas if my starting point was a different game, it would be difficult to imagine the reverse happening.

The real reason I wrote this review is that some people are apparently put off by the presence of miniatures on sprues. It's worth being aware that this is not the lightest game in the world. Spiritually it is closer to the kind of gaming you'll find in RPG or wargaming circles, where the players are used to having a hobby element to their games. These minis in particular are simpler and a bit more clunky than those of competing companies, but the poses are very atmospheric and they do look good when painted up.

The game would be awesome if it weren't for the fact that the plastic miniatures are almost IMPOSSIBLE to glue together!! It ruins the whole game! DON'T BUY THIS UNLESS YOU ARE FANTASTIC WITH THE HOBBY OF ASSEMBLY AND PAINTING OF SMALL FIGURES!!

The books have a recycled, cardboard like feel, but not as if they are cheaply made - they are really solid with thick paper. The printing really pops off the page, and our kids enjoyed reading them. There are plenty of facts without going over their head (ages 4 & 6).
My 2 year old loves these books! Great addition to any little library.

Omg! These are so cute. I use them for bedtime stories. They are not very wordy which is good. They are short and to the point but engaging enough to keep children's attention. There are 3 books. Each about the environment and its inhabitants. Also gives tips at the end how to do better and be more aware. Great segue into being more aware of our planet. The books are pretty sturdy so no worries about kids ripping and ruining them to quickly.

They're made with thick, durable cardboard that is perfect for toddlers. They're easy to wipe off (though not waterproof) and the pages aren't easily ripped.
The colors are bright and eye catching. They're very much geared towards younger children with short, simple sentences and pictures that attract their attention.
I'm very impressed with the cost as well. Most Melissa and Doug products are way overpriced but these simple books are more than affordable at only $8. An excellent value.
I highly recommend these books for any toddler or young child.
Played the original when it came out. This 4th edition is a great streamlined, updated version. I love it and my son picked it up quickly and loves it as well. With all it comes with, it’s a steal.

Free League doesn't mess around, the quality and production value for this game should make Wizards of the Coast hang their heads in shame. The box set has two rulebooks, a set of custom dice, tons of battle maps, tons of cardboard chits to use in place of miniatures and a huuuuge poster map of Sweden and Poland. The new edition rules are slick and pared-down from older editions but easily adapted backwards for old modules (there are no 4th Ed. modules as of this review). The timing for current world events makes games a little spooky these days, some might not like that. Either way it's a fantastic game and definitely one of the best RPG boxed sets out there today (even better than the Cyberpunk Red and Call of Cthulhu sets).
Haven't used it for a long time, but works as expected.
good place to find hard to find or out of date games

Is a very handsome ken doll, with black hair. The tattoos is cool, and very artlike. His clothing is cool also.

They are fun to mix and match and my 1 year old daughter can sit for more than 30 minutes playing with these and literally "scrambling" these eggs around! This game may cost $11, but you wouldn’t be able to tell from the components. The board came already warped. It won’t sit flat on any table. And the game pieces require you to use scissors. great game!  this is the second "cooperative" game we've gotten and we are really liking them.  this one is a matching game, with the pieces being different valuable items, jewelry, golden goblet, painting, etc.  you blindly pick out 3 pieces and slide them behind the secret door.  then you take turns flipping pieces over to try finding matches. when you flip a piece over, its either a valuable item or a clock. if its an item, you flip another piece over to try to find its match. if its a clock, it goes up along the top of the board - once you get a certain amount of clocks, 12 I think, you lose the game, time has run out. the point of the game is to figure out which 3 valuable items have been hidden behind the secret door, by flipping pieces over and figuring out which ones you don't have a match for.  we've played the game probably 6 times and have only won it - figuring out what the 3 pieces are before we flipped over all the clocks - once. its fun though. and its cooperative, so you can help each other remember what item is where. my 7yr old son and I love playing it.

We've had the game for almost a year and we've only played it when it arrived.  Frankly, my 5 year old daughter and I didn't find it as much fun or as stimulating as expected based on the positive reviews.  Main complaint is that the pieces are INCREDIBLY flimsy (has a home made feel) and one has to manage that aspect instead of fully focusing on the important things.  My secondary complaint is that the game is barely more involved than the basic memory card games that cost much less and no where near as challenging as Zoombinis software, which we love.  So, this game is not much fun, not well made, not very stimulating, yet it carries a relatively high price - avoid it!

I guess it's the theme, but I like this game alot more than I should.  It is definitely a kid's game, and it isn't something I would play with just grown-ups, but still.  I like finding all the little treasures.  My six-year-old son has Pokemon cards, Rat-A-Tat-Cat, Guess the Pokemon Game, Castle Keep, Sorry Sliders, Incan Gold, and this game.  Of all of them, The Secret Door is my favorite.  The treasure and clock cards are tiny and thin, and the board is pretty small, so if you need top of the line components you might want to steer clear.

My 7 yr old daughter loves this game and it is fun even for older kids and adults to play unlike some kids' games.  It is basically a memory game with a fun twist -- there are 12 sets of valuable objects depicted on 24 cards.  Three of the cards are randomly chosen and placed behind the "secret door".  The rest of the cards are shuffled, along with 12 cards depicting clocks, and then all these cards are placed on designated spots on the board.  Each player takes a turn by turning over two cards, trying to find matching valuable objects.  If you find a match, you remove the pieces from the board.  If you don't find a match, you turn the pieces back over and try to remember where they were in case you find either object somewhere else on the board (like a typical memory game).  Players work together and can help each other remember where pieces are hidden.  There are also 12 clocks hidden among the valuable objects.  If you turn over a clock, it is removed from the board and placed on the row of clocks at the top of the board.  The goal is the game is to collect enough matches that you can figure out which three cards are missing (hidden behind the secret door).  Players win if they can figure out the missing objects before all 12 clocks are found.  I would have given this game 5 stars except for the INCREDIBLY poor quality of the game for the price.  The board itself is only 12" square and doesn't even lie flat very well.  The cards are made of super thin card stock with no coating, so they will wear out fast.  And the artwork is very crude.  When folded, the board doesn't even fit well into the box -- we have to jam it in to fit.

You wouldn't think this simple, kinda chintzy, game would be a family favorite but it's actually quite fun! The graphics are laughable and the cards are dreadfully thin and flimsy which makes me wonder how durable they will be, but the concept is actually a really smart one. The players work together (which is a refreshing change to the competition aspect of other games) and the idea of having to beat the clock is what makes this so fun. It's essentially a memory game but again, it's the idea of having to find matches before midnight that kinda makes it intense. We play many rounds of this during our family game night. And the secret door is really whimsical. Our daughters like to open it with a resounding "creeeeeeek!" So cute!

The game is fun for children of all ages; however, the small size and flimsiness of the cards really detract from it. Once you lose a piece or two, it is useless. If it were bigger with nice sturdy cards, you would really have a winner here.

My 2 year old has developed a real love for tow trucks and since he already has a huge Brio collection, I decided to go with this one. Like all of Brio's trains and accessories this tow truck and car are excellent quality, perfect for rough toddlers who don't yet know not to throw expensive toys.

The tow truck has a few moving mechanisms that really make it a stand out toy. The hood can be lowered to reveal the engine inside and the tow bed can easily be raised or lowered (raised lifts the car's front wheel axle while lowered keeps all 4 tires flat).

There is a full sized magnet on the back of the tow truck to tow the included car or any trains you may have. However, there is a small, almost worthless magnet on the front of the tow truck as well, and this is the reason I've removed a star from my review. My toddler accidentally discovered the front magnet and now continously attempts to have his trains pull the tow truck. The problem is any time he pulls his trains up hill, the tow truck falls backwards, along with any trains connected to the rear of the tow truck. It's not a deal breaker by any means, but it is slightly annoying considering they could easily fix this. Nonetheless, I'm kind of used to this disappointing feature as Brio does not put a magnet on the front of any of their engines (in comparison, every Thomas brand train includes a front and rear magnet).

This is a definite buy for any kid who loves tow trucks, trains, or both as my little one does.

As a teacher and developmental therapist, I work with children 0-3 and I am never ceased to be amazed at the ingenuity that children have with this toy! It's very versitile! Of course with all children, you have to keep a close eye on them with the small pieces. However, I have found that with careful supervision, I can use this toy with chidren of all ages working on different skills from fine motor skills (grasping, pinching, manuevering the pieces into the holes) to gross motor skills (dancing, moving with the pieces) to cognitive and play skills (naming the parts of the body, putting them in the correct places, making up names for the figure, etc). There are just so many skills that children can learn from this one simple toy. WIth the different pieces, you can take one toy and make it into about a hundred different figures. Children can be creative and make up their own figure (I had a 3 year old who told me her Potato had 3 arms and 2 sets of feet) or be more traditional and keep it to their basic skills. It is a great toy that can be used across all ages. I primarily use this toy with children 0-3, but I have also used it with my older kids up to about 6/7 working on several other skills like their creative and imaginitve skills. I would highly recommend this toy for anyone working with children, whether you are a teacher or a parent or anywhere in between! A MUST HAVE in EVERY toy collection!

Why oh why don't I learn to listen to the one-star reviews?! Several customers said the potato head they received was not as pictured. I assumed (wrongly) that they would surely have updated the product photos to reflect the actual product by now. Nope... our Mr. Potato Head came with a completely different (and slightly scary-looking) mouth, and the hat and glasses were completely different.

That, I can live with, but as other reviewers pointed out, the parts don't stay securely in the holes. Mr. Potato comes assembled in the box, but as I was pulling him out, one arm and one ear fell off. So far, the cover of the compartment in back that holds the spare part (the extra mouth) is staying on, and I hope it continues to, but the entire toy feels very cheap and flimsy compared to the Mr. Potato Head I had as a child, so I don't expect this to score high in the long-term durability department.

I gave it two stars because my toddler likes it except for the parts constantly falling out, but I will likely find myself searching the auction sites for a more durable Mr. Potato Head. I'd take the scary-looking version from the 70's over this one.

I bought this as a Christmas gift for my daughter when my kids were a little over 3 (son) and 1 (daughter). They are now 4.5 and 2.5.

The biggest annoyance with this is more to do with my kids and not the product. They tend to throw some of the pieces off the fridge and leave them on the floor (always a pleasure to step on!). My favorite though, is when they just turn it on and leave the kitchen. The main piece has a pretty quiet hum to it that makes you wonder what is running. It's amazing how my kids can't "hear" me but as soon as I shut this thing off they notice it from another room and run back in to turn it back on. There is also one piece that I recommend you hide right off the bat which is a clicker piece. If repetitive noises bother you - do the same and hide the piece that clicks to save your sanity.

One other suggestion I have is I bought an old metal tray at a flea market and you could attach it to or lean it up agains a wall if you have a stainless steel refrigerator or did not want to have it on a fridge. My kids and I do really like these gears overall. I am a stay at home mom, so I rotate the toys frequently to keep things fresh. This is always a hit when it comes back out.

I bought this because I thought my 4 yr old would love to see how gears move. And this little toy did not disappoint. Not sure if the piece was faulty or if it's a "feature". Overall a decent toy. I would have given 5 stars if it was sold a little cheap.

I have purchased a few sets of these over the last few years (2009-2013) for friends' kids, neices and nephews, from ages 1.5-8. Of course small children need supervision with small parts, but that's nothing new. Basically no matter how young or old, these are a winner. My parents keep a set on their fridge (50+) because they find them fun and basically everyone who steps into the kitchen fiddles with them. Even babies can enjoy watching the motion of the changing colors and patterns.

The largest gear has the motor and the green arrow is what controls the motion; in the central position, it's stopped, flick it either direction and the gear will turn clockwise or counter-clockwise. Set non-motorized gears against the motorized one, and you suddenly have a functioning system. Each gear has a magnet on the back that is strong enough to prevent it from sliding, but not so strong that small hands can't easily re-arrange the gears in any of a number of patterns to get different motion and combinations. The motor is strong enough to move all the gears, but not so strong as to pose a pinch hazard to small hands. This was a concern of mine on my first purchase, but it's not a worry.

These make a nice gift because you can be almost certain the family doesn't already have three of them sitting around gathering dust. Multiple sets can be combined and used together and it only enhances the experience. Also, if a piece gets lost, the whole toy is not ruined. So long as you still have the motorized gear and at least one or two others, it's still a great toy. These are also good for "smaller spaces", as they can be stuck to the fridge or a metal cabinet and don't necessarily need to be "put away" after each use. Plus, this allows for play in the kitchen (within sight) without toy clutter on the floor.

As for durability, they hold up very well to being dropped, stepped on, even tossed to see if they will magnet to the fridge mid-flight.

Although I wouldn't necessarily classify them as an "educational toy", these also help teach kids how gears work - sometimes you place them in the wrong orientation and the gears will try to work against eachother and stall out. But since you can maneuver these while in motion, if you make a wrong move, they lock up instantaneously upon placing the gear, so kids can right away see their mistake and correct it. Just bumping the wrongly placed gear out or removing it, and the others start turning again.

Battery life is phenomenal - even with periodically being left on for extended periods of time, and it just takes 1 AAA battery, so no special trip to walmart to search out that hard-to-find battery when it does go out. We use a rechargeable one in ours.

Smaller hands might have a bit of a hard time with motor skills at first flicking them on or off because the switch is fairily low profile, not the clunky dials and switches they might be used to, so it's not the easiest to grip, but isn't that part of the point? To stimulate new skills? Watching an older sibling or parent using it, they are able to do it pretty fast!

The price point is the biggest downside, but these are a quality toy that will last for years to come. Overall these are a phenomenal product and a great addition to any household.

This is pretty much the same as my review for the Duncan Imperial, but it still applies.

Last summer I went on a mission trip with my church and we decided we wanted to take something fun for the children down in Mexico. My school has recently gone through a yo-yo fad so I suggested we get some Duncan yo-yos. They thought it was a great idea so we bought a combination of Duncan Butterfly and Imperial yoyos, totaling around 60 yo-yos. When we finally got down to Mexico we passed them out to a few of the children. At first they seemed uninterested, as they could not really get the hang of it, but after about a day or so everywhere you looked some kid had a Duncan yo-yo. Even though we didn't speak the same language, the whole rest of the week kids were running up to us saying "Yo-yo? Yo-yo?" It turned out that the adults used to have yo-yo competitions themselves, so they showed some of the older children their tricks. The kids loved them.

The only issue was the price. For a few weeks we searched the web for the best prices and the beat we could find was about $3.12 per yo-yo. We eventually decided to just go to the surrounding Wall-Marts we found more than plenty for about $3.00 apiece. The Duncan Butterfly is a very sturdy and classic yo-yo. It is great for people of all ages who are just starting at yo-yoing. The main disadvantage of this yo-yo is that it's inability to spin very well at all, which makes it great for beginners but horrible at string tricks. Once you have mastered it I would suggest upgrading to a Duncan Mosquito to try out some string tricks and moving up from there. I prefer the Duncan Imperial yo-yo over the Butterfly for its shape as it is easier to control and is less difficult to keep upright.

Conclusion: A very durable yo-yo for beginners, though I would suggest getting something like the Mosquito if you have some previous experience and plan on getting skilled at yo-yoing and I would suggest getting it from your local Wal-Mart or Meijer over purchasing it over the internet. Hope this helps!

P.S. If you have any questions just leave a comment on this post and I will try to get back to you as soon as I can.

I bought these to use myself, in art journals and mail art. They are easy to handle and the box is good to keep them organized. The stamps themselves are made of a kind of plastic - eg not rubber or polymer, so I found that some inks work better with them than others. I found that oil and solvent based inks (Versafine, Stazon) worked best, and neither seemed to stain the stamps. Also worth noting that many of the letters do not produce a perfect impression, because the plastic material has tiny "wells" in it, so you may get a white spot in the middle of your Y, for example. I have a two year old who absolutely loves playing with playdoh. We spend a lot of time molding and modeling clay so I really wanted to like these over the plastic tools and molds available from other sellers. But the fact is that these do not preform as well.
The paint on the wooden stamps is starting to chip a little bit and doh sticks in the grooves all the time. Doh will stick inside other plastic tools on the market too, like extruders (because they have hard to reach grooves), but in my experience, never on stamps. The painted surface on these stamps is not smooth (which is very annoying), and so the doh sticks for that reason too and is harder to clean.
The rolling pins look great but are wobbly, and that makes it very hard for my daughter to effectively roll the doh. She barely if ever uses them.
The cutter is totally ineffective as far cutting playdoh is concerned (I assume it's a cutter).
We love the stamp lids on the small doh cups provided. They stamp well and are fun. They are plastic.
The doh provided is really as a token. Very small quantities and the quality is not something to write home about. Make sure you have your own modeling clay/ doh on hand, if you still decide to order this set.

I purchased this for my son because we are typically fans of Melissa and Doug products and thought he would love this.  The 'stamps' were not sanded and were rough.  The rolling pins really need to be larger, and the playdough is AWFUL!  It was not very plyable from the beginning and dried out within the first couple of weeks, even though we always put it away and sealed it promptly.  There are much better options out there.
I usually like M & D products but this dough is so sticky, the clean up is so difficult. The tools will be useful with another clay brand.

Imensly disappointed with this set. We are huge fans of Melissa and Doug toys and were thrilled to find such a cool looking set. It's poor quality and appears very cheaply made. The stamping blocks are useless because of the cheap wood and craftsmanship.

The wooden toys are nice but I can’t get over the play dough included. It is SO sticky you can’t even play with it - just sticks to all the toys and cakes your hands and makes a huge mess. Two stars only because Melissa & Doug is a great company with focus on quality construction and environmentally sound packing. However this dough is truly crap. It is sticky, it stains, and cannot be removed from clothing, rug, or the crevices in the tools. Expensive mistake.

Buy it for the tools (quality wood rolling pins, cutting tool, and stamps) THROW THE CLAY AWAY UNOPENED or you will spend hours cleaning the kid, tools, rug, and clothes with a nail brush.

We receive a lot of Melissa & Doug gifts. Consistent with other toys we've received, we would never buy this for our little one. We have several issues with this kit in particular - 1) their playdoh equivalent is terrible - it's crumbly (with bits dropping off all the time), hardens easily and lacks the doughy consistency that makes it fun. it's like they picked the lowest cost playdoh supplier to save a few pennies and it definitely shows. 2) we've had the kit for a couple weeks and the paint on the blocks is already chipping. really? on top of that, it's not clear their paint is fully non-toxic, so the blocks were disposed of immediately, 3) the playdoh caps (which are supposed to double as molds) don't really do the job. they are made of cheap plastic and the mold lacks definition. Verdict: A good concept, but a poorly executed product. With concerns about product safety, I can't recommend this to anyone.

This play-dough tool set is super sturdy, and unlike other reviewers, I haven't had any trouble with paint chipping off yet. The rolling pins are great, allowing the handles to stay still while the cylinder rotates. The block stampers are popular with the kids, but the triangle one tends to get dough stuck in it. However, just stick a pen in there to get the remains out, it's not that difficult. My biggest complaint about the product is the play-dough that they included along with the containers. First, the play-dough is tough, rubbery and a very small amount, so you might as well just throw it out. It's cheap and easy to make my own, so it's not a huge loss. Second, the containers are very difficult to open once snapped shut, nearly impossible for a child to open by themselves. I'm thinking about tossing the cups and just keeping the lids for the stamps.

Addendum: A few days after I wrote this review, a rep from Melissa & Doug randomly chose us to follow up on our review. That's some hands on, unsolicited customer service! My son is just 2, and this set of tools is a perfect fit right now. There are enough textures and activities to keep his interest for 20 minutes solid, but not so much that it's too overwhelming for his attention span and motor skills. He especially likes the animal stampers on top of the dough containers and the roller that makes a "waffle" pattern. I like that the tools are wood-- just feels good-- it doesn't have a pre-determined program like many of the playdoh sets-- make ice cream, make popcorn, stick plastic dinosaur parts into lumps of dough... this is really about exploring in an open-ended way with just enough suggestions to be a good teaching tool.

I agree that the dough isn't very abundant or the best texture I've seen, but it's enough to get started, and I haven't had trouble with it sticking as other posters have. It has a pleasant, slight baked-goods smell and I like how vibrant the green color is. I also agree that the rolling cutter doesn't cut very well, but it does make perfect "train tracks," which is even better in my son's opinion! The moving parts move very easy and my son has no difficulty using them. They provide him hours of fun while playing with play doh making shapes, stamps and patterns. Anpther great product from Melissa and Doug!
My twins fight over these, they love it so much!  You'll need to bring your own playdough though; the small containers included don't give enough to keep the kids busy very long.
Nice quality tools for play dough.  Easy for my 2 year old to handle.  Colorful tools, good variety of interesting shapes.  Encourages pressing, rolling, and cutting skills.
Nice quality. My 3 yr old loves using this set to play with her play dough. Arrived on time. Good price.
Nicely and neatly packaged. Very good quality. My son is two and love playing with it and using all the tools with the play dough.
Our family has been using these for nearly a year without any issue. They are holding up great and are a quality product.
Our grandson plays with play doh several times a day; this is on his work table, and he uses the various pieces a lot.  His mom is also pleased with this product.
Our niece loves using these tools with her clay and Play Doh sets. The tools are sturdy enough for her younger sister to be able to use them when she is old enough.
Part of Easter gift for grandchild.  Great and will last.  He uses this with all his play doh at 3 1/2 years.
Perfect for use with playdough and clay. Playdough doesn't stick to the pieces at all and they make great shapes and patterns. Highly recommended!

Perfect little set! I bought this for my 2 year old (who is a bit young, but I was over anxious). He loves to "roll" the playdough out and stamp it with the stampers. It came with a decent amount of playdough which is great! It comes in a nice wood box to keep it all togther.

Perfect set for using with Play-Doh. This set is well made & is way better than the plastic ones you see out there. My kids love the different designs you can press in the Play-Doh. My daughter loves to play pretend making & cooking food with it & my son loves to use the designs to make buildings.
Real wood, quality set.  Exactly what we wanted!
Really enjoyed playing with it.
Saw some unkind reviews but ordered anyway. Love Melissa & Doug products and granddaughter is older so think she can handle it.
She will love it!
The kids lived them and they are good quality
The kids love to play in the kitchen.  These kitchen tools help them learn how to cook.  They often help their mom make food for dinner.

These are very worthwhile for children, even adults like to play with them.
They can be used for generations if you take care of them.

These tools are well made and easy to use for toddlers 18 months on up.  My granddaughter plays with them constantly, and the little wooden tray to keep the tools in is great - would be nice if it had a lid, tho.
These toys are a great starter to any childs play-doe collection.  My boys love to use them.  They are small and easy to handle.  I like that they aren't plastic and will hold up longer as a result.

These wooden molds and tools are keepers! I've bought many plastic sets for our 4 children, but this one is the best yet.
I like that it has a wood box to keep them in and that there are 3 rolling pins. The modeling dough amount is small, but the tools can be used with any type of playdough: we've also used it on commercial and home-made.
I already plan to keep it for my grandchildren in 20 years.
This is a very nice addition to the Play Doh Fun Factory Deluxe Set, a gift for our 2 and 5 year old grand daughters. It's probably a bit too sophisticated for the 2 year old, but she's learning.
This is an excellent creative toy.
This is such a cute little set. Hours of fun for little ones--with minimal mess. the play dough doesn't stick to items. The colors are vivid. Well made.
This is the best play dough tool set I have purchased for my children. My daughter (3 1/2)  loves it and plays with it often. These tools just help her imagination go even farther.

This item was purchased for my 2 1/2 year old. He loves this much more than the play-doo kits.  He really likes the textures the rollers make, and he likes the wooden blocks that press shapes into the dough then he will smash those shapes.  But like all Melissa & Doug everything is made out of wood, it is better quality than the plastic stuff you get from play-doh.  It isn't as fancy as their presses and shape makers but my child enjoys this kit more.  Also to me the dough seems to be softer than play-doh but it will dry up the same and become tough just like play-doh  and there isn't anything you can do about that.  But this is a great kit and for $15 I have not been able to find it cheaper, unless you come accross it at a discount shopping facility where they may have it for $10.  But that is more luck than anything.  So I recommend this item for children....even their parents!

This set is nice quality from Melissa and Doug. My toddlers love pushing the Doh into the patterns and making all kinds of shapes with this. I highly recommend it to foster creativity in young children.
This was shipped to another state and it has been reported to me that they are enjoying their gift! :)
We typically buy Melissa and Doug toys as birthday presents and we kept one of these for our daughter. She loves all the toys to play with play doh. It's a great activity for her on a rainy day.
We used this with regular Play-Doh instead and it's awesome
We were able to purchase this during black Friday deals. As expected from Melissa and Doug all items are quality!  It's been a good addition to my son's play doh.
Well made.  As usual for Melissa and Doug toys.

What can I say about this toy?  It is a Melissa & Doug so you know the quality is top notch.  It will last for generations.  Great product.  Our granddaughter loves this when she gets out her playdough.

You can't go wrong with Melissa & Doug products. I've purchased this same set multiple times for the children in my life. My son has had his for six years and it's in great shape. Once he grew out of an obsession with play dough, we started using it to sculpt Crayola's air dry clay.
as always, melissa and doug product was a success to buy and use. my kids had so much fun using it!
everything as expected, play dough mess as usual :)  I am never disappointed with Doug & Melissa products.
fun times
grandaughter loves playing with it
great little set!  The rolling pins are kinda varnished so the play dough doesn't stick to them...
great price
my 3.5 year old really enjoys it.
okay fun for the kids
perfect for crafts
well worth the cost. Loves of fun items.
what's not to love?  Melissa & Doug have great quality items!
wish it came with a container, other than that it's durable and fun. oh and not too much playdough is given.
yay
모두 나무로 되어있고 가격좋아서 우선만족,  오른쪽 동그란건 도우가 칼라별로 조금씩 들어있는 케이스 이구요. 뚜껑부분은 동물모양을 찍을수 있게 되어있어요. 무늬같은건 괜찮은데 자르는 칼 한두개 정도 구성이 더 추가 되었으면 하는 바램입니다~
The dough was sticky and had to be thrown out.

I love Melissa & Doug but seriously this 'play dough' is the worst ever. I purchased this set more for the 'tools' than the Play Dough....dont scrimp, go with regular, good old fashioned Play Dough.....it doesnt crumble like this!

I was very disappointed in the playdo portion of this set. All together, it wouldn't even make one canister of regular Play-Doh. Very disappointed that we have to spend more money to get them something to actually use with the rollers and stamps. The only good things in this set are the rollers.
Not easy to clean and very sticky. We usually love Melissa and Doug products but this one not so much.

The play doh is a little too sticky and the wooden parts get the clay stuck inside.  Easy to clean, but would rather have bought a plastic set.  2 year old loves it though, so we're still happy we have it.
Bought this for my 2 year old, the only thing that hasn't held up is the little wheel on the cutter. But it was easily fixed with some hot glue. He loves it, especially the stampers

I bought this for my granddaughter and mainly for the rolling pins.  The quality is very good and everything works as expected.  The Play Doh like substance provided is not nearly as good as the real thing. That was not a concern as I did not need nor actually want the modeling clay that came with it.  If you are expecting Play Doh you will be disappointed.

I bought this set having read the previous reviews about the bad modeling dough, so I was fully prepared for the compound to be exactly as it was - junk.  Unlike other reviewers, our modeling compound was really tacky (not dried out) and just made a big gooey mess.  However, I already knew you don't ever expect anything other then real Play-doh brand to behave like Play-doh.  So, that aside, I paid the toy price knowing I was paying for the wooden toy pieces, and I still believe the set is worthwhile.  My favorite feature is the rolling pins actually roll - just like grandma's!  Many of the plastic rolling pins in other sets are a single piece, but these rolling pins function just like the one I use for making biscuits.  The pins are a little smalls, but kids hands are small.  My kids (2 and 4) have really enjoyed them.  My son (4) also like the rolling cutter.  Neither child has taken to the wooden block/stamps yet, but time will tell.  In general, I believe most Melissa and Doug items rock.  This one would probably be better served if they took out the modeling compound and dropped the price slightly, but I do not regret the purchase.

My 2-year old son really likes this play-dough set. The rollers are a bit small but the handles turn so he can still use them pretty well.  He really likes the stamping blocks which are perfect for little hands.  We haven't used the play-dough the set came with because it just doesn't seem as good as the stuff we make at home.  All in all this is a great set though!
My two year old loves this set, but I was disappointed to find a better Melissa and Doug set that includes more tools at Target for the same price.

Over-all, a lot of fun! The play-doh itself dried out easily and the stamps leave behind some of their paint. The lids/molds are also a little flimsy. The rollers and cutter are awesome and great quality!
The rollers are a little small. The Alex Toys are little bigger.
The tools are great, but the play dough that comes with the set stained my countertop surfaces. Be careful!

Got this for my 2 1/2 yr old Grandson and he loved it. He loved rolling the Play doh and stamping it and he played happily for a good hour or so. I also like the fact that the product is made of wood rather than plastic. I would recommend.

I was thinking about the other cheaper plastic set but so glad I went with this one.  It's substantial so it's easier to roll or stamp the playdough with and I had no issues with the playdough that came in the set (of course it was a smaller quantity than the regular tubs).  I like the case it comes in as well - makes it easy to keep organized and ensure the parts do not get lost.

This toy was a Christmas gift for my two year old granddaughter. She loves it and plays with it often, sometimes for a few hours. The things she makes is very interesting to see, very educational for her. Toy is durable for many years to come. Would purchase this again.
When my grandson received this (we Skyped while he opened and played with it), not only did he enjoy it, so did all the others in the room!  Great quality item for hours of imaginative fun!
my daughter is only 2 and loves playing with this set. I love the fact that it is durable wood.

Parents please be cautious and check your dough for this set. In the brand new sealed blue dough there looks to be fiberglass or metal in it. All other ones seemed fine but be careful. Please do yourselves and your littles a favor and double  check!

This set is not that great. The tools are good but not at all usable. The dough is very limited and bit on the harder side and difficult for a kid to play with. It is better to buy the Play dough kit instead of this.

When will Melissa & Doug figure out that toys like this need lids?! I love the high quality of their products, but they don't do play-doh well. The actual dough is very small and not enough for my 3 year old to make the shape and designs he wanted. Plus the rolling tools don't roll smoothly. He had a hard time with it.

We have several other M&D toys and they all end up losing peices, because although they come beautifuall packaged, as soon as you open it the small pieces escape and they can't be contained.

I cooked my grand-daughter up a batch of home-made playdough and introduced these tools. With a little enouragment, she took to them immediately and played with them for about 10-15 minutes at a spell, several times a day during my week long visit. I DEFINITELY recommend home-cooked playdough, because it is so much softer to the touch and won't grind into carpets the way the commercial brands do. Just google "homemade playdough" and you can find many excellent recipes.

This set develops fine motor skills.Also, Little B LOVES to organize, so she enjoyed putting all the pieces back intto the slots.

My daughter loves to use the tools with her dough, but the dough that is included tends to stick in all the corners and is very difficult to get out.  I had to wait until it dried to get the excess off. Otherwise this is a good product. My daughter likes to pretend cook so the rolling pins are great for her.

I bought this for my 3-year-old to use along with her kitchen baking toys and she loves it. She loves baking and rolling out dough for cookies and pies and this simulates that very well. She really enjoys using all the rolling pins and observing the different patterns they create as well as with the stamps. The first time she opened it she played with it for over an hour. The only reason I didn't give it 5 stars is because the modeling dough is a bit difficult to manipulate and has a funny smell. The smell doesn't seem to bother her at all, maybe I'm just used to the more popular Play-dough that I grew up with. This modeling dough is not quite as soft and she needed a lot of help at first to get it rolled out. In the beginning she was a little frustrated with it but we worked it out. I think I will be buying a pack of the other dough to go along with it.  I have to say that the dough that comes along with it does have it's advantages to being a little stiffer, perhaps this was the thinking behind it when creating, it doesn't get stuck in all the grooves of the stamps and rolling pins which makes this toy extremely easy to clean up. I also love that it is made of real wood. The pieces are very sturdy and everything I've ever bought from Melissa & Doug does very well standing the test of time. Overall, I would definitely recommend this to others that have little bakers or children who enjoy playing with this type of material.

Best toy ever! This occupied my 19mos old granddaughter for several hours a day while visiting over the holidays! The little modeling dough cans are small and portable to take to restaurants. You get everything you need in this great assortment. Rolling pins and shape cubes to press into the dough. I also threw in some cookie cutters for an added element.

The set includes a good assortment of fun accessories to amuse most toddlers. And all tools are made of sturdy wood. Unfortunately, the modeling dough tends to fall apart and the colors stain easily. Also 3 of the 4 containers didn’t close properly. I contacted the company and they immediately sent a replacement! Melissa and Doug is a reputable, quality company, which I trust to make safe products for children!

The wooden toys were fine and my grand daughter enjoys using them.  However, the molding dough included is terrible.  After it is handled and warm it sticks to everything!  Will toss it in garbage and refill the containers with either home made dough or more popular brand.  I should have read the reviews and completed the exchange of dough before the set was given to child.  What a mess to clean up!

These are awesome little tools to use with Play Doh!  I like that they aren't heavy enough to hurt when used to bonk your sister over the head, but they do a great job rolling out your Play Doh and/or leaving lovely patterns in it!  The vivid colors make them very pretty to look at, too.  I recommend them.

The tools are sturdy and nice, and the value for the kit was totally satisfactory -- so I gave 5 stars. Wound up making some playdough with my toddler. The first batch (from a normally excellent art blog called Artful Parent) was a disaster and the second batch (from the My Heavenly Recipes blog) turned out great. This set is the best I've ever seen. My grandson and his parents are very pleased ! Good quality, unique, lots to experiment with. Thanks for making such a great activity for the little ones !

I would definitely recommend buying Play-Doh or making it at home. Since it is easily made at home, that wasn't a big issue for us.
