The included play-dough here is very bad quality, getting gummy and sticky after not many uses. And the cubes with shape cutouts get the dough stuck in the cracks, making them very difficult to clean. I wish I had bought something different.

I had always heard Melissa & Doug toys were made in the USA, so when I saw them on sale, thought I'd buy some for my grandchild...

Didn't know they are made in China now... with all the problems with dead dogs & cats from bad food/treats, with lead in paint used in toys and drywall/sheet rock problems from China (besides why help support China)... had I known, I'd have never bought them... didn't know until the presents were opened so I can't return them...

They burned well in the fireplace (hope the smoke I didn't kill any birds flying overhead)

Will never buy another toy from this company
My daughter loves this toy!  She spends hours making designs and animals out of clay.  Also, clean up is a snap with included wooden case!

The product itself was excellent however the packaging came mangled up and ripped open. Would not recommend if you are looking for a collector item. Product is still functional and in fine condition. Also fully functional.
Arrived quickly but had a crease on the package.

The figure arrived with the box open and the little wall accessory piece completely missing. I might have kept it anyway, but the light saber was about to break. I ordered the Count Dooku action figure shown here, but received the action figure from the cartoon animated series instead.  It looks silly when all the other ones are based on the movies.

I got this for my son so he would have Count Dooku in his collection, but when we played together, I found myself playing with him more. Dooku is awesome. The item showed up on time. I now order my Star Wars characters exclusively from Amazon.

this toy is cool. he comes with two lightsabers like anakin hangar duel. i found him... i couldnt believe it. i opened it when i got home. this toy is fun to play with. i dont know what the point of the darth sidious hologram is, but i like it. there's one problem. this is an extremely rare figure. so if you see one get it before it's too late.

Count Dooku, the baddest villian with the dumbest name ever in a film.  But while Christopher Lee can pull it off, this toy doesn't really come close.  Bearing just a passing resemblance to Lee, it is almost impossible to get the toy to balance correctly.  In addition, the red lightsaber blade is not removable, as all the blades in the current line of figures.  His cape does not stay on his shoulders, and his limited posability gives you very few options for positions.  I hope that a newer version of this figure will improve both the likeness and the articulation.

Ahh once more, the detail and pose and design are just all OOOOOOH wonderful!! But the only problem is the packaging design. They are in cards (plastic covers with cardboard backing), not boxes.  The figures themselves are too heavy to be hanging on a cardboard hook so that the hook gets worn and the cardboard backing is rounded and warped.. EVERY ONE OF THEM!! So if you're picky like me (bc I like to collect, buy and sell MINTY FRESH items), you might have to be patient and persistent in finding a mint package. I got mine thru amazon.. and they STILL were warped!! So I returned them. ^_^;

What can I say, fantastic. The figure is much bigger than the pictures do it justice. Very happy with it. Since these figures are impossible to find since they are part of a new line testing phase I would encourage anyone to scoop these up while they can before it's too late. This and the Darth Maul are sure to be big collectibles down the road. I was a little dissapointed that I received one with a small crease on the upper right hand of the card but it's not that bad to damage the value all that much. Like they say "Having one is better than not having one at all". And certainly in the world of AOTC collecting that is the case. This is a must have for any serious AOTC collector. You'll love it!

They may not be as posable as the industry-revolutionizing Todd McFarlane Spawn toys, but the new Star Wars Unleashed figures certainly owe them for a bit of inspiration. If it weren't for the success of action figures aimed towards collectors with an eye for sculpting, size, and sheer "geekiness", Hasbro might never have dared to try something like the Unleashed line for Star Wars. They could have just stuck to the old stand-by, smaller figures; not that there is anything wrong with those classic, retro toys, mind you. It's just cool to see big, statuesque, artsy SW pieces after all these years. Jango Fett is a good first choice. He is exceptionally detailed and with him, you essentially get two for the price of one when you consider his son, Boba, is right by his side. You can actually choose to have Boba wearing his cape or not wearing it. Simply removing Boba's head enables you to switch back and forth. Fun stuff, to be certain.
Hasbro is merely tapping into the potential of the Unleashed line, however. Starting with Episode 2 characters is an obvious and logical choice. Just imagine, however, when they start dipping their toes into the waters of the classic SW characters. Darth Vader, Chewbacca, Tusken Raiders, Bossk...all would be intriguing choices to get the Unleashed treatment. Let's hope we see more of this new line, sooner than later.

Anything I say about "Jango & Boba Fett Unleashed" will not do justice to what I consider to be the #1 collectible action figure available in the entire Star Wars Collection.
It is almost as if this diorama set of two figures are alive. The detail in both characters is outstanding. I especially love the face on Boba. His arm is reaching up towards the sky with mouth open and teeth showing; waiting for his father Jango to protect him from the evil force. Who ever designed these characters is a genius.
There are a few people in this world that are not big fans of Star Wars. Needless to say, I haven't met anyone with those feelings. However, I'm not so naive to think there are a few. What's my point, you ask? Simple! If you are a fan of action figures of any kind, you will want this set for the following reasons:
1. Outstanding Detail
2. So life-like your imagination will think they are real people.
3. For the serious collector the packaging is detailed to the point of tree limbs surrounding the plastic case.
4. Forget about NUMBER 4 - I rate this a 10 + + + + + + + +
The imagination that went in to creating "UNLEASHED" is outstanding, to say the least. I was only able to purchase one because it was the last in stock. It is available now, but don't wait too long. This is the only set I don't think will make it in to the secondary market. WHY? I can only imagine people wanting to keep them for themselves. Heaven forbid...a selfish thought. Oh, maybe you'll see one or two on line, but the price will be way up there.
Get this before my prediction comes true. I've been on target with all of my other reviews; so I have no reason to believe I'll be wrong on this one. Sure, it's hot now...but wait...STAR WARS UNLEASHED WITH JANGO AND BOBA FETT will be boiling in the not too distant future. What else can I say? Out of the 100 or so action figures I own, this one is by far the BEST.
Respectfully to its creators,
Steve

I purchased this game for a little girl's 4th birthday..I thought it was a traditional Bingo game with pictures but it's a bit different. I bought this at a local store for Christmas for my son who will turn 4 in a few months. We have played it now three times in the last few days and even my daughter who turns 2 tomorrow is able to play...and kinda good at it!
Each Zingo game card has two sides, a green and a red. The red is supposed to be more challenging so we have only played the green side so far. I play the dealer, who slides the red box to reveal 2 of the yellow plastic word tags at a time. The mechanism is easy to work and both my kids can do it but it's easier if I'm the dealer to keep them from being distracted. Also the tags are difficult to slide back into the dispenser one at a time through the slots. Both kids help put them in when the lid is off - they are rounded one one end and flat on the other making it easy to see which direction they face.

Play is fun and it only takes about 15 minutes for someone to win, though with two toddlers we play until everyone "wins". My son is just starting to read and I hope that continued play helps him with his reading to link the word with the picture. My daughter easily recognizes each picture, even "smile", and calls them out by name, though she is not reading at all. They don't always remember what they are looking for and sometimes call out the name of whatever tag comes up even though they don't need that to make a match, but again I love the reinforcement of the picture names and words.
My husband hasn't played this with us yet because we've played it while he's at work but I love that this is a game we can all play as a family. I look forward to a family game night!

Santa brought us Chutes and Ladders and Candy Land too. Chutes and ladders was confusing and disinteresting to the 2 yr old and took the 4 yr old quite a few turns before he kinda got the idea. My five-year-old and three-year-old love this game! It's like Bingo, but with pictures/words. You slide the "dealer" forward and back to reveal two tiles at a time. Each tile has a picture and a corresponding word. Each player has a card with various pictures/words corresponding to the tiles. If you have that picture/word on your card, you say the word out loud, and then put the tile on your card. The winner is the player who fills their card first. Each card has a red side and a green side. The red side is for older players, and the green side is for younger players. It appears that the red side utilizes only a subset of the total available tiles, making it a bit more difficult to fill your card (i.e., all of the potential tiles are represented on the green cards, but only some are represented on the red cards). When a player fills his card, he yells "Zingo!"

We were introduced to Zingo by one of my daughter's friends. My daughter instantly loved this game. Sliding out the Zingo tiles through the Zinger is fun for the kids and I am always amazed at how fast their reaction times are in recognizing the tiles that they need. We didn't read the instructions at first and didn't realize that the yellow side was less competitive (i.e. fewer conflicting tiles per player) as sometimes our daughter would get frustrated because someone would take "her" tile - but I guess that teaches her the value of taking turns and being a gracious loser ... (I wish!) It helps to play the yellow side to reduce frustration by the younger players.

The game doesn't take too long, so the kids don't lose patience and it is fast paced and easy to clean up. The box says ages 4 and up, but I have bought this for so many birthdays for 3 year olds and they all love it. It is a great and unique gift idea for any preschooler.

We've been playing "zingo with a bing" (I think we've got that backwards, but now that it's named at our house, that's what it's called) for three weeks now, and we all think it's great.  It's very sturdy and easy for both a 4 year old and a 2 year old to maneuver the dispenser, and the shape of the tiles makes loading fairly easy.  No batteries are required, and it makes no noise - a nice break from computer-y games.  The pictures and words make it easy for all ages, and it's fast to play- so we can play 20 times or just 2, time permitting.  I was skeptical at the price, but I'd definitely buy it again.
Wonderful, fun game for all ages.  My 3 year old loves this and it challenges her to think quickly and react when she sees her cards come up in the dispenser.  Awesome game!
Wonderful:) Kids love it!

Zingo is a game like Bingo - you have to get all the pieces in the row to make ZINGO. The system is very easy for kids to use on their own. I like that my kids can get this game out, set it up, and play it without adults. It good for kids to learn to take turns and follow instructions independently. This game certainly helps build that skill.
best game for young kids. not too hard and keeps them active
definitely a nice game for my 4 year old.
fun game and my son plays with it all the time
fun game for my 3 year old and 7 year old. this is great for a family game night. the 3 year old has a little trouble with it. it is better suited for ages 5 and up.
good game
love this game! give as gifts for birthday parties because its always a hit!

my toddler loves this game.  she just now starting to like to play board games. until now her favorite was busy town, but now zingo is her favorite game.  i like the small yellow cards. they made of plastic, so they will hold up to abuse quite well.  the game doesn't take to long to play each card, and it has very easy set up.
perfect for kids, this is very sturdy and can be used by any age. very very happy with the buy.
this game is great for any preschooler to start learning to play with others in sit down games. you can make your own rules or play by its. sturdy enough for little hands.
Game arrived used!! Had to rush to buy another birthday gift.
This game is ok, my 3 year old was not all that interested. The little game pieces get stuck in the slider a lot which is frustrating.
Great product!  Great vocabulary builder and fun for kids young and older.  I bought it to play with my 3 year old and he loves it.

I am 35, my DH is 28, and my kids are aged 5 years and 2 years (29) months. We got this game around Christmas (a couple of months ago) and it has been a hit ever since. The baby is not good at shouting for the zingo pieces by name, or yelling Zingo! but she grabs the pieces she needs and if her board is filled first we prompt her to say Zingo! and give her the win. We all really like this game, but the kids can also play it by themselves. The only drawback is the pieces--they are easily lost if you are not vigilant about watching the kids with them. This was worth the money spent. Other games the baby can play (with little guidance or assistance): Hiss, Uno Moo, and Brown Bear, Brown Bear. My son could play Memory at her age, so we assumed that she would be able to as well (she hit all of her milestones before he did), but she wasn't ready for it (even playing with only half the tiles) so we will wait to reintroduce that game when she is 3 years old. I am thinking of trying the Curious George Beach Discovery game next...

My son, who just turned three, loves this game! He's learned a few new words, but the best part is that it's a game the whole family can play together. Some of the characters are a little odd - the ghost, for instance. My son had no idea what it was or what it was supposed to be. Parents will need to explain some of the images, but I think that just adds to a learning play time.
The only drawback has been that when the red sliding case is full of the Zingo tiles, it keeps getting stuck and it becomes hard to slide it forward to release two new tiles. Sometimes one will slide out and the other will get stuck mid-way through.

We received this as a Christmas gift and have played it a lot - it's easy and quick and we like to take turns being the "dealer" - our four-year-old loves to hand out the cards and check to see who's winning.  If you like this, you will also like Smatch from the same company, and vice-versa!
As the numerous positive reviews reflect, this is a great gift for kids. Loads of fun.
I LOVE this game and I am almost 27 years old!  I play it with my two boys (3 and 5).  We play double cards to keep them challenged.

I bought this game for my 3 yo daughter, because she loves playing with our "SmartMouth" game, which is the same format (it uses letter tiles, instead of pictures). We have already played it several times since she got it for Christmas, and just lastnight we played with Grandma, Grandpa, myself and my husband. We all had a really great time,and it's basically just a matter of paying attention and being quick enough to call out your "tile" if you have it on your card (like Bingo). She has even played this with her older boy cousins (ages 4 & 6) who enjoyed it as well (they usually prefer Transformers and more electronic games). I bought this on sale, but would definitely pay full price for it :)

I bought this game for my 4 1/2 year old for Christmas and she LOVES it!!  It's a lot of fun and can be played with just two people or with a large group.  It's also not a long game, so it is easy to play a quick game as a reward before bed or whenever.  It is easy to learn and to play and all the adults who played with her over Christmas break also really enjoyed it!  Would HIGHLY recommend this game as a fun way to spend time with your child!
I bought this game for my three-year-old niece and everyone in the family loves it.  Her seven-year-old brother will play with her for hours.  The adults can play without getting bored.
I had a 4 year old, 6 year old, and 11 year old playing this game this weekend, and they all loved it!  They were playing for candy, and had a blast.

I have a 5,5 & 8 year old (girls and boys) and they love this game.  It's actually refreshing to see them be entertained by a board game like this as compared to their normal Ipad, Iphone, computer games, etc....This one is especially good for kids in that just beginning reading stage since it identifies words with the pictures so when they are the Zingo Master they can call out the correct picture on the tiles.  What a great way to play bingo from a kids perspective.  No wonder there are so many great reviews for this game.

I purchased this for my 3-year-old daughter.  Little did I know that it would become one of the most requested games on family game night.  My 5 and 8-year-old sons enjoy it, too.  It is simple enough for the youngest, but not too baby-ish for the oldest.  The game goes rather quickly and keeps their attention.  They love working the slider that reveals the tiles.  The only drawback is it takes a little bit of time to correctly reload the tiles in the slider, but it's not that big of a hassle compared to the fun the kids have playing it.  Highly recommend!

I think we have played this game well over 200 times in the 3 months we've had it. My kids (2 & 5) LOVE it, as well as every other child to come into our home. We've had kids up to ages 10 play it and love it every bit as the younger ones. This game has helped us create some great family memories!!!

I'm not sure why this game was discontinued as it's probably my preschooler's favorite game.  It's teaching him sight words, strategy and it's super fun.  My husband and I play with him in the evenings and this is one of OUR favorites to pull out of the closet.  It's not too repetitive or boring like some other kids games can be.  We love this game and bought a couple for birthday parties as well!

I, as a grandparent, gave this game for a Christmas gift for our 4 year old grandaughter.
It was an immediate hit and I enjoyed playing it with her as well as our other 3 older grandchildren. Not only is it fun, but it also helps to improve memory. So it is educational as well. I highly recommend this quick game!
My 4 year old REALLY enjoys playing this game.  I highly recommend this.  He received this as a gift for his 3rd b-day and it never gets old to him.  Great fun game to play w/ all the family.
My 4-yo niece really enjoys the game & is learning at the same time. Definitely a quality game & durable construction.
My boys (ages 4 and 5) love, love, love this game.  And they can play it by themselves without the help of an adult.  Great fun and good educational toy!

My four year old daughter received this game for Christmas and we've played it at least everyday since then. Zingo is a unique twist on Bingo that uses picture (and word) tiles instead of letters and numbers. One player is the dealer and calls out what the two tiles are (example: Panda, Cat) and the first person who has that picture on their card to call out "wins" that tile. This is where the game gets fun and in our case a little loud, with everyone calling out to try and get the tile before someone else. The first person who fills in their card and yells ZINGO wins. It's nice to have a lot of beginning reader words/pictures and the game cards are very sturdy and are two sided. The tile slider is easy to load -- even for kids. I hope that ThinkFun comes out with supplemental cards and tiles, as I could see that these tiles might get a little old after awhile.
My four year old and my seven year old nieces really enjoy this game -- the older girls don't think it's a "baby" game and my four year old is recognizing words and is "reading" like her older cousins. ZINGO is a great addtion for family game night.
My four year-old son LOVES this game and wants to play it all of the time!  The cards and tiles are very durable.  I would definitely buy this for a gift!

My friend bought this game for our 3 years old son. We've been playing it several times a day, everyday and we have this for about 3 months now (you can imagine how many time in total, we played). It has 6 thick board and sturdy tiles, I'm not worry about my son would ruin it because he often got too excited. It has a good educational value too, the first couple time he played, he was all about win and grabbed all the tiles (that matched his board) first, not following the rule. But now, he learnt to be a good sport and he did often win without cheating.

Yes, I recommend this game for kids. It's fun for me too!

My husband got this game for my son (a 3 year-old). He loved it and has been playing with us 3-4 times per day. The game is simple enough to be easily understood by a kid of his age, and interesting enough to keep everybody happy playing it. We have tried other games sold for his age, none has attracted as much attention or provided as much fun. Zingo is a great game and the people behind it really know how to create a satisfying and enriching experience for children and parents.
My kids love this game and it helps their memory
My kids love this game!  My 7 and 5 year olds had a great time with this educational game.  They love the tiles and how they come out of the holder.  This is a complete blast.

My son received this as a Christmas present just before his 3rd birthday in 2008 and he has loved it since!  It's a great matching game that also teaches taking turns, patience and sportsmanship (if two people both call out the name of a picture tile, being gracious and letting the person who called it first get it, then maybe next time, they'll let you have it even if they called it first again).  The only thing that bothers me is how cumbersome it is to reload the tiles.  We've had lots of family laughs and good times with this little game!
My son still likes to play this, 6 months after he got it. It was his absolute favorite toy for 2 solid months. Highly recommend

One of my daughter's friends bought this for my daugher's birthday and she loved it so much. We started buying it for  people we knew. It is easy to play and very educational and can be used with young kids because they can match the pictures up.

Sat down to play this game with my 7 & 5 1/2 year old boys and an almost 3 year old (B-day next month) girl and they all thoroughly enjoyed it.  It is a very durable game - cards won't bend and the pieces are big enough and made of sturdy plastic that won't easily break either.  Game is listed for 4 and up, but my little girl had no problem playing and keeping up.  They all took turns running the Zingo machine to dispense the tiles and all helped with reloading the tiles.  A previous reviewer said that was tedious, but if you let the kids do it it's a great way to improve fine motor skills.  For older kids, they can play more than one board at a time.  I didn't notice a big difference btwn the red and green side of the boards.  This is even a game that a child could play on their own, by just matching.  Game also helps to improve memory so that you can get tiles before your opponent.  Another plus is that the game isn't too big, so you won't lose storage space.  Highly recommend for ages 3+.  My older kids (11, 9 & 7) love playing this with the younger ones because it is straight forward with no complex rules.

THINK FUN ZINGO is definately FUN! I ordered this game to have at "Grandma & Grandpa's" house to keep on hand for the pre-school Grandkids! And what FUN AND educational it is, the younger 20mos. has fun matching and learning what taking turns is all about while the 4 1/2yr. old loves to take charge and set up the playing pieces and being the(bingo, if I may)ZINGO caller! The game is well constructed and worth the money for sure!!! And now I guess I must order a SECOND ZINGO to keep at Grandma & Grandpa's house SINCE they begged to take my game home with them! And you know Grandparents "Well O.k.!"
The game is entertaining as well as educational.  It provides word association to the pictures on the tiles as well as enhancing memory skills.  My 4-year-old son loves it!

The object of this game is simple enough for little ones to understand which allows parents to play along with their children.  Our 3 year old grandson loves Zingo and has been playing with his brother and cousins for several months now.

This game is excellent for children with autism.  It teaches them to wait for their turn, matching, reading, etc. It can easily be used as a learning tool without the child feeling he/she has been given a task.

This has quickly become by 4 year old daughters favorite game.  It's just like bingo except with site words so she is learning as we play. The best part is working the nifty tile dispenser each turn!  Great family game and I will also buy more for birthday parties!

We got this game for a 5th birthday gift and love it.  Now my 4 year old plays it and beats us all the time.  It is a really fast paced game that builds focus and memory skills..... highly recommend for preschool through elementary school.  Great game to get family together it keeps adults entertained too

We have had this game for several fun-filled years! We used to play with our now 11 and 8 year-old boys back when they were about 4-7. This is a fantastic game that the children and adults in our family enjoy! It is easy to set up, fun to play, and extremely durable. As I mentioned, this was used for about three years by two boys and it still has all the pieces and is in excellent working order. We are excited that our little girl (who is two), is almost old enough to play! I love that it is something I don't mind playing, either. The rules are simple, the game is easy to play, it doesn't take long to complete a game, and best of all, it is fun! I would recommend this to anyone with children between 3-7!

We picked this game up for a steal at a local thrift store 2 weeks ago. Not a single day has gone by that one or more of my children has not played with it. My 3, 4 1/2, 7 and 8 year old children all love this game. My 3 year old will sit with it by herself and match the tiles to a card. She can also play it (and win!) against her older siblings. I didn't think my 8 year old son would be that interested but he takes it out and gets the other kids to play at least every other day. We are a gaming family, and have lots of games. My two oldest can play complex games like Memoir '44 and Ticket to Ride, and Zingo still holds their interest for a bit of quick fun. Get it; you will not be sorry!
We play this game over and over. It is fun for myself and my husband to play and my 3 year old loves it. This was a great purchase!

Wow, this is my child's favorite toy ever. We try to quit after 20 minutes, but at her insistance we've played for two hours straight. She sleeps with one of the cards under her pillow and wakes up wanting to play it again. It's insane. Being 3, I can't believe how focused she is when she plays the game. She studies the 9 pictures on her card and intently watches the tiles to be drawn so she can grab them quickly. I like that it's fast paced. She's doesn't like Candyland or Chutes and Ladders yet. I think it's because she gets bored of taking turns and counting (which she's still learning) and she doesn't quite understand the rules. With Zingo everyone plays at once and the rules are really simple. A child can actually play this game as well as an adult once they get a little practice. I think that makes it fun for the adult because you can enjoy playing the game and not have to worry about "letting" the child win. They can win without your help and know that they did it on their own. There are duplicate tiles of all the pictures which is nice because the child doesn't get frustrated when someone grabs the tile they need first. They know that another one will eventually come up. Another plus is that the game won't be ruined if you happen to lose a tile or two.

My only complaints....
- The tiles don't go back into the dispenser through the little slots well. You really have to jam them into the slits to get them to go through. We just pile the discarded tiles up and have to take the time to get them all facing the right way before reloading the dispenser through the large opening.
- This is trivial, but I'm tired of the pictures already (maybe because we play it so much!). I wish they had more cards you could purchase. I know they have one to practice counting, but we're not ready for that yet. More cards to expand on vocabulary would be great though.
- Also trivial, but some of the pictures are on the cards much more than others. For example, many of the cards have a heart or a star, but very few have a tree or an owl. It just means that when the tiles are revealed you discard lots of trees and owls and they end up feeling like wasted fillers.

I bought this game for my 4 year old and it turns out, my 4 year old and 2 1/2 year old play this game together, with the parents, and even alone.  It is the game they ask for every night.  A non-reader's version of Bingo, it is easy enough for the younger children to match the tile to the picture on their card.  Would recommend this game.

We got this as a present for our 3 yr old over six months ago and it has been played almost everyday since! Excellent game. The whole family loves it and it has been even brought on roadtrips and weekends away. Can't speak highly enough about the game. Only negative comment is that it would have been great to include a word only side as well as a picture and word side to encourage word recognition...never mind still an excellent product.

We got this for our almost 4-year-old for Christmas.  It has been used more than any other gift we gave her.  She wants to play it every night.  We have used it as a teaching tool to learn how to play with others, how to concentrate and pay attention to the next set of "cards" and to react quickly, how to be a good loser and a good winner, and how to quit playing without throwing a fit.  My son, age 2, is learning to match the pictures up and say the words.  He does much better than I thought at his age, but he isn't as interested in sitting for long periods.  I know he'll love it when he gets a little older.  My husband and I are not game people, but we can tolerate this one.  We can play about 3 games in 15 minutes, so that's not too much to ask.  :)  I can also play it alone with my daughter: I "deal" and play a card at the same time.  Highly recommended!
Zingo is a great game to start young ones out  Builds terrific learning skills and is a great way to spend good family time.  Great twist on the old game of Bingo.

My 3.5 year old son has not really shown a great deal of interest in reading books. He doesn't have the patience to sit and draw for more than 10 minutes. Even play dough won't keep his attention for long. I truly believed that this game would be no different, and that he would be off playing with something else once the game got underway. How wrong I was!

Since the very first day we opened the box, he has been engrossed in this game, and wants to play it all the time. He's surprisingly good at it too, and my husband and I sometimes have a hard time beating him. We thought we'd have to hold back and let him win, but he can win all on his own.

We have been anxiously awaiting the day when we could all sit down as a family and play a game together, and I am thrilled to find one that can hold his attention.

The game is very well made, and seems rugged and durable for rough-housing.

My 3 year old son got this last year for his birthday and it is still a favorite.  He will carry a step stool and climb up  himself to get it off a high shelf.  It has helped build his vocabulary and he has become very quick at grabbing the tiles.  This will be a great treasure for your child too!
My daughter got this game for her 3rd birthday.  She and her 4-year-old sister love to play this game daily!!  Now I am buying more to give as birthday gifts to friends/family.

This is a game that my daughter plays while at speech therapy.  When her teacher told me about it and how much fun it was for my daughter, I bought it for us to play at home.  It's a great way to work on vocabulary and pronunciation instead of drills and flashcards.  Bingo is always fun to play and the dispensing system of Zingo keeps it mess free.  I do wish there were bigger cards with spaces for more tiles.  The games are over pretty quickly, especially with just two players.  Still, my daughter always asks for one more game, so she enjoys it.

I completely underestimated how much my daughter would love this game. I saw a similar game on a lightning deal before Christmas, but it looked a little advanced. So I took a chance on Zingo because it was so highly rated both on Amazon and by other mommy bloggers that I follow. I was not disappointed. My 3 1/2 year old LOVES this game. We've played it at least twice a day every day since Christmas. We've taken it to Nana's house to play. We've taken it to the neighbor's house to play. Sometimes, I'll find her in the living room with her stuffed animals spread out playing it with them.

What I love about this game is how simple it is, both in concept and construction. The cards, "dealer" and tiles are all very sturdy. I also love that it is a game our whole family can enjoy. Her father and I are big board game people, so it's fun to find a game we all share. And it's a game that can grow with her. As a bonus, she's learning to read some of the words on the tiles. I've already told my husband this is going to be our stock present that we give to every kid in our lives that is part of the preschool crowd. Buy it for your kid or a kid in your life...you won't be disappointed.

I bought this for my 5yr old daughter, and it's perfect for her age/ability. She has started reading and loves board games. It's very entertaining and it's helping her learn new words. I've tried it with my almost 9yr old son and he gets bored very fast because he knows all the words; the only thing that keeps him playing is trying to win. So I'd say it's for 5-8yr olds, not 8 and up.

Game is extremely well made and durable - since the chips with the pictures are made out of plastic I can see this game lasting a long time.  I bought this for my 5 and 8 year old - all in all they seem to like it, but one suggestion I would make to anyone who is buying it is have at least three people - two kids playing and a parent to make the chips come out. When allowing the boys to play on their own (so both have cards to fill and one of them is operating the chip releaser) the child releasing the chips "hovers" over them so they are usually the one to first say and grab the chip...which results in fights.

This is a cute version of bingo. Young kids invariably dig it.

Putting the tiles back in the dispenser when your done (or as you go along) is a mild annoyance, but most home bingo games are worse. The sturdy cards, indestructible tiles and mostly reliable dispenser are generally an improvement over other bingo games. Which is not to say that your kid cant knock the thing over and spill the tiles, just that its a lot more kid-proof than other home bingo games I've seen. Similarly, the dispenser can jam, though this will be very rare if used by the adult, and even kids can get the hang of doing it reliably -- it is generally reliable, not perfect, but good enough.

This differs from bingo in only one way: once the tile comes out of the dispenser, you have to grab for it (or call it out), because if two players both have it, whoever gets it first (or calls for it first) gets to put it on their card. This is a minor variation from bingo, BUT do not underestimate the difference it can make if your playing with very young and/or headstrong kids. Whether this is a big deal or a non-issue depends on your kids, but its certainly not going to be as low key as traditional bingo regardless.

I knocked it down two stars because of the rule variation. While my kids really enjoyed it when they were 4-6 (boy) and 7-9 (girl) we had some difficulty at times with the boy's behavior, and it wasnt always the relaxing experience that bingo can be. You could probably use pennies as chips to make it track the rules of traditional bingo, which with hindsight probably would have been a good idea for us.

Beyond that, its an excellent implementation of bingo for kids.

This game is great. Since the day we got it, it has been played with all the time. The kids love being able to yell "Zingo" when the win. The design is great since you can keep all the pieces neatly tucked in the dispenser.
A great fun, educational game for game nights and rainy days!
The age that was advertised on Amazon was different from the age on the box. It is for younger kids not for 8 years old.

My family absolutely loves this game.  Our three year old especially loves it.  The main issue with it is the difficulty of reloading the dispenser. [[VIDEOID:753c4d352c3a7f6a2636dd12a9e7d72e]] 2 1/2 and 4 1/2 year olds playing Zingo.  You can see that even the 2 1/2 year old can play and can set up the game.

While I've been discovering how chalks are not created equal, neither are chalkboard surfaces. This is actually perfect to chalk your board with. It's inexpensive and if you have a large board(like I do), and a few smaller ones(like I do), then this is the perfect choice because you will go through a lot of it. Chalking your board makes it easier to erase any chalk. Just wash(I use dawn dish soap but any household cleaner will work unless it's wall paint) your board and rinse thoroughly and dry. Once your board is completely dry, take your chalk side ways and start drawing in wide strokes, covering your whole board completely. Then take a dry cloth and rub/wipe the chalk into your board with a cloth. I use my sham-wow's that I've cut into smaller pieces. You will end up with chalky look, but it will be easy to wipe off any chalk. I know, you will lose the pretty and clean look, but if you rub it good enough, it will still look good. I've learned the hard way to do this with all my boards from the very expensive porcelain chalkboard to the cheapie easels. It works wonders. Guess you could just wash it every time you are done, but I don't have time for that and this way the kids don't get frustrated. I only wash if the boards get gunked up.

"Over and Out" is a fun little "adder" with a couple of interesting twists. Nothing really original about the concept: just keep the count from going over the "Tip Top" number. Go over the Tip Top, and you lose the round: not really a revolutionary idea there. Nothing really intriguing about the "action" cards, either: you can reverse the order of play, draw a new Tip Top, or bring the count right to the current Tip Top. (The latter being a huge help when the Tip Top number is 66 and the count is in the teens.)

What gives the game a fresh feel is the chips: Each player starts with 4 chips of his color, and all players are dealt 4 cards to start. However, if you go Over and lose a round, you lose a chip--and there's no way to get it back. And when cards are dealt for later rounds, you only receive as many cards in your hand as you have chips in front of you. So if you're down to two chips, you only get two cards to play with. Run out of chips, and you're out of the game; last player standing wins.

(One "house rule" I recommend is that if you've down to your last chip, and thus have a one-card "hand", you have to keep your one card face down in front of you until you actually play it. Since you have no choice but to play the card you've got, it doesn't matter if you see it or not. It adds a little bit of suspense to the game, particularly if only two players are left in a one-on-one, winner-take-all final hand.)

One downside to all the fun: more players can make for a VERY long game. If you want to play a full 8-player, 4-chip game, give yourself a *lot* of time to finish, because you might end up having to play 31 rounds to determine a final winner. This is not a game you can whip out and finish in five minutes, since every extra player adds four more rounds to the game.

However, if you've got a bunch of friends who want to spend a night at the table chatting over a friendly (or even not-so-friendly!) game of cards, this might be the perfect game. There's not a lot of strategy involved, play is very straightforward, and there's not too much to keep track of beyond what the total count is. It requires some concentration, but not a lot of heavy-duty thinking.

Overall, not a game to write home about, but certainly not the worst way to pass an evening.

The second set of deluxe figures to Hasbro's "Star Wars Original Trilogy Collection" is made up of four characters from "The Empire Strikes Back". The first is the Dark Lord daddy himself, Darth Vader.

This new figure of Vader comes equipped with his cloth black cape and his red lightsabre. The sculpt is classic Darth Vader all the way.

The front of the new figure card is from the "Empire Strikes Back" lineup from 1980 (back then,it was actually a revamp of the "Star Wars" card with the new "Empire" graphics). The back of the new card is different from the old, with a brief "Star Wars" nostalgia blurb and a checklist of the other three figures from the "Empire" line (Lando Calrisian, Yoda, & C-3PO) as well as the other two sets from "Star Wars" & "Return Of The Jedi".

The Darth Vader figure is cased, like the first four figures from the "Star Wars" set, in a plastic star style keepcase with the clear "Star Wars" logo in raised lettering on the back.

These figures are more detailed than the regular figures and if your a "Star Wars" you would be foolish not to pick these up.
Great gift for any original Star Wars fan.

Developing crawling - this ball is weighted at the bottom and moves around a bit making your child move/crawl to get the ball. It doesn't roll really far...more like a couple inches so it's not really frustrating for your baby.

Battery life - excellent! I've only had to replace the batteries once and we've had it for 3 yrs. It also shuts off it no one plays with it (love that feature!!)

Negatives - really I don't see any!

Additional notes -
My 3 yr old still plays with this ball with our son (1 yr). Since the ball says what the animals say and what the numbers are on the lights I think its great. It gets her talking to my son.

My 8-month-old daughter loved this ball, and we thought it was a great toy for her, until she cut two of her fingers on it pretty badly.

She was playing with this ball one day (with me in the room) when I suddenly noticed that the ball had blood on it. I quickly took it from her, looked her over, and saw that she had bleeding lacerations on her third and fourth fingers of her left hand near the fingernails. I held pressure on her fingers with a tissue, and although one finger stopped bleeding after ten minutes, her other finger continued to bleed slowly for over an hour despite my holding pressure on it. By this time we had called our pediatrician and were preparing to take her to an urgent care center if necessary. Fortunately the finger finally did stop bleeding, after which we had to do our best to keep her from re-opening the cut somehow.

I examined the ball closely and in the area where the blood was most concentrated I noticed a sharp corner next to one of the buttons where the larger pieces of the plastic sphere come together. The plastic doesn't line up evenly here, creating a very sharp corner (see photo, which shows the sharp corner and some blood). In fact, there are several spots on the ball that feel similar. I am confident that this is where she cut her fingers because 1) that is where the blood was most concentrated, and 2) I had just put her down on the floor (a large soft rubber play mat) with the ball two minutes earlier, right after changing her diaper, and the ball was the only thing she had played with during those two minutes. I even checked the floor around her to make sure there was nothing else she could have cut her finger on, and I found nothing. When you run your fingers over the sharp spots it's easy to see how a child could get injured by them.

Obviously I was extremely disappointed by this event because we have purchased several other VTech toys for our daughter and our nephew and we have generally been quite happy with them.

We got the Quercetti Georello Tech kit as a Christmas gift for my son, who is in third grade. The kit consists of different sized gears, links that you can put together to make a chain of sorts, cranks, segments, all of which can fit together in various ways to create mechanical objects, be they specific or abstract.

In short, my son is special needs. He loves to take things apart and figure out how they work, he likes to improvise making his own toys to express his imagination and interests.

After scouring Amazon's catalog I chose the Quercetti Georello Tech kit because I wanted something that would fulfill his craving for taking apart, building, and improvising, and would also not end up frustrating him in trying to put something together. I saw another reviewer mentioned getting this kit for a special needs child and after seeing how happy they were, decided this was the one.

I should probably point out one of my son's conditions is ADHD. He can hyperfocus on something if it holds his interest, otherwise forget it, and he can and does get bored pretty quickly.

The Quercetti Georello Tech kit passed the litmus test in our house with flying colors - he played with it for hours every day during his winter break. We'd gotten him a few other toys and the kit was the only one he consistently returned to every time. We were REALLY impressed.

His favorite thing was to create robots... and I'll confess Mom and Dad had some fun with the kit too! I've been tempted to get another kit so we can have more parts to build and invent. Highly recommend.

I initially purchased the Quercetti 55pc Kaleido Gears for my 3.5yr old to make sure that he would enjoy this type of toy.
He loved it so, although this set is slightly out of his motor skill range for his age; I bought the larger (and more expensive) 266 piece set seen here.
One small element that I find very pleasing is that all the parts from the Kaleido gears can be integrated with this set. Their gears are toothed the same and the dimensions/attributes critical to integration within each set are common to both sets.
It would be quite safe to assume this is true with all of the gear type sets available from Quercetti.
The box that Georello Tech is packaged in is durable enough for continued storage, and comes with a plastic carrying handle. This is also true of the 55pc Kaleido Gears set.

I am very careful and thoughtful in every purchase I make for my son; considering point of manufacture, materials, durability and long term influence (ie: educational value or lack of).

Yes, we've thankfully gotten beyond the tight business suit which the 2000 version had (thus now enabling President Barbie to decisively move around her Oval Office) but it does not look like a 21st century women's powersuit either. Rather, it looks like something that might be worn on a 'vacation' (including the inevitable staged appearance).

Since President Barbie is also presumably encouraging girls to think about both photo ops AND policymaking, she should have been given a powersuit wardrobe. This will enable her to be taken seriously when she faces the Barbieworld Congress to advocate on behalf of girl rights.

In a world where grown women now have a wide variety of attractive business clothes to choose from (yes, including some oulfits with pants!), it seems weird that a toy publically intending to help kids think about their futures is fixated on past stereotypes of what is 'dressy' and what is 'casual'. Pants do not automatically mean casual lounging anymore.

Since Barbie also has pantsuits, surely the design team could have done better with this product. I like the other materials she comes with, but think that President Barbie is due for some serious wardrobe consulting.

I just got and then put together this guy today and I have to say that this is easily the best Gundam model I've put together to date. While not as detailed as a Real Grade or high-quality as a Master Grade, this kit is still incredible. It's basically an oversized High Grade kit, so if you're new to Gundam models and want a good looking, bigger model, this is the one to get. That said, it's still a great build and incredibly sturdy. It also looks great without any real touch ups and minimal sticker use gives it a much more solid look, although the plastic tends to look a bit cheaper, so detail aficionados may want to coat it first. After finishing it comes it's best feature; It poses and plays great. I've built six or more Gundams of varying grades and series in the last few months and this is the first one to be this posable since my High Grade 0 Gundam that is as smartly and widely articulated as Astray. It's solid like an SD figure, so it's limbs and such aren't loose, but poses like a High Grade. Alongside all of that he comes with a full set of hands you can swap out, none of which need removing of parts on the hands themselves to use (the exception being with weapons and such). Astray also comes with a rifle, two beam saber (storable on it's back) and the trademark, big ole' Katana, all of which look great. It's an excellent model for anyone looking for a cheaper alternative to it's Master or Perfect grade counterparts, while wanting something grander than it's High Grade.

Toe-in, and toe out balance on the fronts needed to be close to neutral on my M-05 as my chassis is heavier with lead added lower in the chassis, and lightened with graphite stiffeners up top, and upgraded servo horn, steering linkages, aluminum king pins, steering knuckles, aluminum steering hubs. I'm sure if you get a solidly made one, it's plenty of fun. I'd planned on letting me and the office pals have plenty of corporate/client frustration sessions with ol' Bozo for many a day ahead. So after ~20 minutes (prior to which you spend 15 minutes blowing the lungs out of your mouth to fill it up), you have one sad looking deflated Bozo. Tried the included patch kit to fix it, but the patch just won't meld with the seam. Just leaks as if it wasn't on there. So, needless to say, I'm returning it. As always, Amazon's no-hassle return policy makes it much less frustrating than it could be. So bravo to Amazon, boo to the manufacturer who went cheap with shoddy material to make this Bozo. I am not sure what all the hubbub is on here about Do-A-Dot markers being super-staining. While we have not actually dumped the ink out of these things to ensure they won't stain my carpet, my daughter is definitely not "careful" with them and we've never had a problem. They certainly come off a kitchen counter, a wood floor, an IKEA desk, windowsills, and a coffee table. They also come off her skin just fine. And so far I have not encountered stains on her clothing which tells me laundry detergent is at least getting it off her sleeves/cuffs.

Not that I would recommend doing these on carpet, or giving them to a hyperactive child, but they are SO much fun, it's hard to not recommend them. My four year old, six year old, and even my seven year old enjoy them. They are very different from regular markers or paints--a great experience of their own. And I give this set in particular five stars because the regular Rainbow set is missing pink and purple, which most little girls really want. I'm not sure they "shimmer" in any true sense--they don't have glitter or sparkles in them--but the silver and yellow-gold colors are nice to have.

Just FYI, the purple, yellow, and blue are NOT repeats from the Rainbow pack. They are different shades, so I appreciated that.

Kinda cheap, but don't feel jipped on the price. It is intuitively easy for my 26 m/o son to use the compact size remote The little thing is 4wd witch 1 axis control for each side independently. Never-the-less he opened it Christmas Day. I installed new batteries 1.6v each tested before installing. He dropped the remove once from about 2 feet. Suddenly the remote LED stopped coming on indicating it was not xmitting. I disassembled the remote w/ about 7 screws and found a poor soldering work throughout from the battery compartment to the controller board. I put it back together with my soldering tool. Presto fixed!!! Then the 1st use in the bath tub the car is not water-proof around the axles(pretty much means its not good for the water like advertised) I disassembled the car and found more poor soldering work. Tested voltages throughout circuits and deduced the motor coils had become flooded. We let the toy dry out overnight and put it back together...Presto it is working. Most parents wouldn't go this far but my son has so much fun with it and is very simple circuitry. Over all good value for a 24 m/o to maybe 7 y/o!

Pro: Dollhouses are an all-time classic favorite toy that will generally not disappoint most children including the Twin-Time Dollhouse showcased above. The imagery in the Dollhouse is very realistic including fabric curtains, and it includes parents and twin baby dolls.

Con: The cost, which was $230. in August 09 (and this didn't include shipping!) is not commensurate with the quality, i.e., this dollhouse is NOT wood, but plastic. Although I like the dollhouse, it was definitely over-priced!

Overall, I would have given this dollhouse five stars if it wasn't for the high-end cost. You can find an equally engaging dollhouse for far less--even dollhouses crafted in wood.

I had always thought of Tyco as the "gold standard" of R/C vehicles. My son had an old Tyco (Rebound, I think) that was handed down from a friend. That thing took years of abuse before the remote antenna got broken off. The car would still be working today if that hadn't happened!

So I was looking for another RC car for my 8 year old. We've tried several others, but none measured up to the Rebound. I THOUGHT this one would be it. I had reservations about the $90 price tag ($60 for the vehicle and $30 for the battery...SHAME!). But this thing is TERRIBLE! Shame on Tyco! Initially, the movement was awkward, thanks to the hard plastic exterior (no rubber wheels) and the flopping "tail". But I thought that the shape-shifting feature would make up for it. WRONG! It changed shape maybe 3 times before the gears apparently broke.

I took that thing back to the store for a refund and I'll think twice before buying another Tyco!

DO NOT buy this toy! Honestly, I would give this toy zero stars. First off, they may clearly specify you have to buy the special Tyco battery, but the problem is if it a special battery, from tyco itself, it should be included IN THE PRODUCT!! Having to shell out the extra $30 is absolutely stupid. Now a days, I triple dog dare you to find an electronic device that does NOT include the batteries it needs. Now lets talk about the Megahertz... We bought 2 of these on the same frequency... One will not work when the other is on. TYCO DOES NOT WARN CONSUMERS ABOUT THIS AT ALL (until you call their god-awful customer service). Mattel needs to either start including a battery or lower the price on the toy itself. The battery probably costs 10 cents to make. Tyco or Mattel products will never enter this household again until they stop decieving consumers. All you folks who gave this toy 5 stars must've just gotten tired of their kids yelling, because there is no justification for spending an extra $30 buying a battery that should be included in the product. I now have a useless $100 hunk of plastic and Tyco wont do a damn thing about it.

This is a great toy, nicely made, feels good to touch, I don't mind looking at it in my living room. But as a teaching toy it has just one trick, and is really suited to a much younger age range than you might think. Even kids who can't name colors can tell them apart, and very young children can "count" even if they don't know that's what they're doing. So the main, and only, learning objective of this toy is easily accomplished by many children well below two. And then they're done. I know that bright wooden pieces are good for lots of other things if you've got an imaginative child, and that's what we'll be moving on to now, as my 27 month old sorts this puzzle out in about 30 seconds.

I highly recommend the Lauri Shape and Color Sorter as a substitute. There are many more things you can do with that one. My older children all got good use out of it throughout their preschool years.

Got this as a birthday gift for a toddler, knowing from personal experience that Melissa & Doug toys are pretty cool. The packaging was not opened at the party. A mere two days later I got a message from the parents saying the paint was flaking off the toy! It hadn't even been used by the birthday boy yet.

I asked them to give the toy back to me so I could replace it, of course. Once I saw it, I was honestly surprised (even after their description) at the amount of paint that was flaking off the blue blocks at all the edges - just by handling the blocks in a normal manner, nothing rough like a kid might do - so, I did something I NEVER do, and emailed the company about it.

The next day I got reply from Melissa & Doug asking me to give them a call so they could get some more information. The customer service rep I spoke to was pretty surprised and appalled at my description of the paint flaking, particularly since this toy was right out of the package. Without having any technical experience in the field, she could only hazard that perhaps the wood wasn't treated properly before painting? She is looking into it using the number off the bottom of the toy.

In any case, she wanted to replace the toy right away, and even gave me suggestions on other age-appropriate toys they had available. She shipped the new toy I chose directly to the birthday boy, one that was actually a bit more expensive than this one it's replacing.

I got my Dad to order this variant of X-23 for three reasons--one, I love the X-Men; two, I LOVE Logan; and three, purple is my fav color:)

Okay, let's get down to the nitty-gritty--first of all, X-23 (real name Laura) is a clone of Wolverine in the Marvel Comics. She's apart of the Weapon X Program, and the same scientists that bonded adamantium onto Logan's skeleton and his claws bascially attempted to make another Wolverine from some of his slightly damaged DNA (that they just HAPPENED to have:/).
These people failed 22 times in a row...
Until with number 23, they succeeded.
The end result was a baby girl with two bone claws on the back of each hand, and one claw on the top of her foot...and she went through the adamantium-bonding process when she was I think 7...
So, basically yeah; Logan's little clone had the worst childhood any of the Marvel people have ever had:/
That aside, now for the pros and cons:)!

PROS:
First off, there are two different X-23s that come in the Comic/Build-AFigure pack: THIS variant of X-23 comes with the head and shoulders of Apocalypse (who, trust me, is a REALLY big dude) like the black one, but this X-23 is in a dark, plum-purple suit; with combat boots and wrist gauntlets in the same color.
She also has 25 points of articulation.
Her hair is long and black, and as the adorable Nightcrawler put it in the comic that comes with it all, "Logan, she has your eyes."
And actually, as weird as it sounds--considering she's plastic--Laura really DOES resemble Logan in the face.
Her face is not only just cute, but it just LOOKS like a kid's face; and her body isn't the typical sensory overload of huge thighs and chests that most Marvel women have--it's like a young girl and realistic.
The funniest thing is that her lips are in this cute, sassy pout that also looks like she's saying "IMA GONNA GETCHU, SUCKA" XD...
And with her foot and hand claws, she does just that--I seriously spent my lunch break yesterday just playing with her and putting her in different poses:)!

CONS:
I got Laura in the mail a week later than her due date, but that wasn't so bad--what bums me out is this:
When I opened her, I realized that even though her elbows and knees are double-jointed, the top joint on her left elbow does NOT move.
At ALL.
I tried bending it gently, then not so gently, then I did Figure Realm's hot water trick and it STILL wouldn't bend.
That was why I said she only moved in 25 places; of it weren't for that she would move in 26:/
Other than the elbow, X-23 doesnt really stand straight--but I guess it's just because she's really meant to be posed crouching down with her claws in the "IMA GONNA GETCHU, SUCKA" stance...XD..lol...

Other than that, I give this Daddy's Little Girl five stars!

--By HGT

a game for the whole family that can be played everyone against each other or even as a team to reach an accurate conclusion. great to help anyone think. young ones can learn to reason and may find more enjoyment teamed up with an older player.

As a fan of both Sherlock Holmes and games, I had high hopes for the game but it really was not much fun and I would not recommend it.

I was super excited when I received the game thinking that it would be like another Clue game, but I was wrong. The quality of the tokens are horrible, one actually broken when I received the game. The "Skeleton Key" and "Scotland Yard" cards are also cheaply made. To pay $16 for a poorly made game is just not worth it.

I grew up playing this game with my family in England. Some of my fondest memories are sitting around the table battling my brothers and parents in a game of whits and deduction.

A lot of the other reviews compare the game to Clue, there really is no compasison this is a far superior game.

I was a little dissapointed in the quality of the game pieces and the detailing in the playing board, the same game in England seems to be of a much higher qaulity and detail.

That being said however, for $15 I would buy this game again and again. It is hours of fun and is a fanastic way to help young children and adults develop a keen sense of deductive reasoning.

I remember playing this game when I was 7 years old and loving it. I would recomend it to any parent who wants to help their child grow up to be a smart and reasonable adult.

I am sure the manufacturer made a good product in their eyes. Problem,it is not described properly. The Chicken Coop is Die Cast Metal on a "tree" which takes very careful trimming and is labor intensive in order to assemble. Who wants to spend hours carefully cutting and triming and fitting small metal pieces just to add realism to your model railroad? If the "Coop" was molded in PLASTIC like most kits it would be a breeze to assemble,fit,and paint. Don't get me wrong I don't mind a little extra work and the product is a great concept but not when casted in metal. If you make a mistake you are dead in the water. While plastic molded items can be repaired and modified to suit your taste. This is why I gave it one star.

I bought these as teaching aids to help my kids learn about different animals around the world. Concepts are so much easier for them to grasp if they can hold and play with the animals they're leaning about, rather than just reading about them in a book. The animals are well crafted, the pieces have been nicely painted and they're fairly detailed which helps them look more realistic - the tiger has fur, the hippo has wrinkles, and the crocodile has armor. They also have their names on the underside in case you need help identifying what they are. Most of the pieces in this set stand up on their own fairly well, although some are a little wobbly. The zebra in this set could be a little better as well. Its stripes are smeared on one side and dusted to look more gray than white.

The only thing I find a little cumbersome is the tube itself. While it's great for storing, it's compact size and odd shape makes it difficult to get the animals in and out - the rope handle also seems to get in the way. Other than the somewhat troublesome tube and the paint job on the zebra, the animals in this set are great and I will definitely be buying more of these collections.

Confession: this is meant to be a toy for my nearly two-year-old, and YES, I will give it to her :), but I have not yet had the heart to wrap it. I'm not generally a stuffed animal kind of person, though I do feel that they are important for kids to have, for with them, your children can imagine all kinds of things and get a little comfort, too (especially if they really connect with the toy). My inner-child has connected with this owl. I will update this review once I see how my little one responds to it, and the reaction it elicits from my seven-year-old.
But for now...
Here's what I have noticed about this beautiful owl. He has those large, bright, penetrating, wise-looking eyes, and they feel tightly secured (I have tugged at them, as I am anticipating that the eyes will get a lot of attention from the little one). His beak is pointed, petite, and spotted with flecks of black. It looks sharp (though it's really not) and is made from a firmer kind of material. His wings have extremely limited flexibility (they cannot be raised to pretend that he's flying). His feet are made from the same material of the beak, which enables him to stand--yes, you can knock him over--but a slight tap won't do it.... stable little guy.

What I love: the exquisitely soft feel of his belly: very soothing to the touch. But, that said, he's not one of those stuffed animals whose bodies conform to the needs of your arms. I guess you could say that he's not really a cuddle creature, but one who is meant to be stroked. Still, he is compact, which will make it easier for my little one to tote around.

Concerns: The hair (meant to be feathers) around his tiny but well-defined ears is wispy, and MAY be pulled out easily by a child as young as mine. Also, since he is meant to be spot-cleaned and not placed in the washing machine, I will have issues if she drools on him. Still fell in love with him, though, and thought that this might be a subtle introduction to learning that there are, for instance, different kinds of "birds" out there.

I actually think that my seven-year-old is going to be drawn to him, and I believe he would be more appropriate for that age group (assuming your child likes owls), especially if your kids are fans of the [[ASIN:0439884764 Guardians of Ga'hoole Boxed Set, Books 1-4]], or are Harry Potter fans who do not feel that they MUST have Hedgwig.

Again, I will update, but my final words are: he is a gorgeous creature, and I am enchanted by him!

My son has loved this puzzle since he was 6 months old. I was excited to give it to him at Christmas. He has put it through the ringer in the last 3 months as he plays with it every day. I have thought all along that the clunky animals and barn pieces were wood until I was wiping it down with some soapy rags. That's when I looked closely where the paint chipped off and saw the edges were getting saturated...it wasn't wood it was pressboard?! I couldn't believe anyone would make a toy that goes into mouths invariably out of something as foul and hazardous as particleboard!!! This is not ok. Now I have to take away my sons favorite toy! I have told my dad about the puzzle and he is cutting out some teak scraps that he has into the shapes and I 'll paint them since I'm pretty good at that stuff and my dad is a great carpenter. Its worth the trouble for my sons favorite toy, especially since i want to keep this puzzle for future kids too.
Why don't Melissa and Doug charge a couple bucks more for pine or poplar...they can use rejected, warped wood...its so frustrating what some companies do to make a buck...its sick when unsuspecting parents and babies suffer from their corporate bottom line. I wont he buying anymore of their puzzles like I had intended to. I will just stick with used puzzles on ebay or something. Such a downer Melissa and Doug! Now I 'm wary of all kids toys.

Bottom line:
If you don't care about your kid putting toxic resins and ingesting particle board, or if your child is over putting stuff in their mouths and banging puzzle pieces to bits, this is a fun little puzzle. My son has gotten a ton of play before I had to remove it, I will be having my dad copy the pieces and maybe the whole back as well so my son can continue enjoying this product safely.

There are matching pictures beneath each pieces . The board and the puzzle pieces , all are hand painted which is the best part I like if this puzzle most of all. The puzzle pieces contains a barn,cow,horse,goat,sheep,pig, duck with ducklings and chicken with chicks. The chunky pieces are well made to be handled by soft little hands. This puzzle not only  made our daughter interested for other puzzle games but also developed her matching and problem solving skill. Best first puzzle from Melissa and Doug.

I got this puzzle on a lightning deal so the price was just perfect. I had had my eyes on this series of puzzles for a while now so I was thrilled to see them featured! I was debating between the peg puzzles and the chunky puzzles but ultimately decided on the chunky for a few reasons:
-Though more expensive, the thicker pieces can hold up to more beatings
-Pieces are thick enough to stand on their own so can double as wooden "imaginary play" pieces (we use them with blocks)
-Thicker pieces are easier for little hands to grab onto- as opposed to grabbing onto the tiny pegs. I know Melissa and Doug sells puzzles with large knobs, but I felt my son would outgrow those too quickly and this way I would get the most use for my money.

I love the bold colors of the farm animals, their smiling faces, and I love that the pieces have the right amount of weight to them. Unlike some of the other Melissa and Doug toys, these don't seem to just have a sticker on the front that can get peeled off (or sucked off since at this age everything ends up in the mouth. The edges of the pieces are painted and smooth.

In terms of age, I love that such a wide range of ages can get use out of them. I got these for my son when he was 9 months old, so obviously he was a bit young to be assembling the puzzle. At first he just took the pieces out from their places and now, at 11 months we go through and practice animal sounds. Eventually, he'll be able to do the puzzle himself. (Note: I know the recommended age for this product is 2+ years. Use your own discretion as a parent to make the right choice for your child)

I am a grandmother, raising a grandson in a vastly changed world since even my kids were growing up. Everything is high tech, computerized or geared to keeping a child still. I understand- sitting still and learning is a good thing. It is also a very hard thing for a child with ADHD.
Kids are too sedentary nowadays anyways.
He had a bike. Not crazy about skates just yet so I was really wasting brain cells trying to find something that would make us both happy.
That's when I began to think back to my own childhood and the things that made me happiest. My parents weren't rich, times were hard but I do remember the year myself and three of my cousins got that red, metal, wagon. We played with that thing until the wheels fell off and of I not mistaken those wheels got fixed a few times.
Such good times are associated with little red wagons that I could not wait to give those same memories and activities to my grandson.
All that being said: it was irritating as heck to put together. One might want to tell somebody that you have a part that needs greased. Also explain that those lock nuts or whatever they are called to hold the wheels in place will move, one one hundredth of a second before you swing the hammer, resulting in a bruised finger and not just a few swear words.
All in all tho- this is a purchase I will never regret.

This is the second electronics kit I have worked on after completing the 'Elenco AmeriKit Learn to Solder Kit'. Definitely a good second project. The first 'Learn to Solder Kit' taught the basics of electronics and provided simple tools. So when I got the Rolling Message Display Kit I already had a basic soldering kit to work with and decent skill with soldering components to the board.

The first thing I realized with this kit is that it would be more difficult and complicated than my first intro kit. The quality of materials seemed better and I could see the components would be closer together and cramped on the tiny board (unlike my intro kit which included a large board with tons of open space). But I felt up to the task!

The instructions were clear and easy to understand, though minimal. I really had no problems at all while assembling the kit. Honestly it was so easy I worried I had done something wrong and wondered if it would work at all! But after completing the kit it did work. I added a battery and voila, the LED lights lit up with a Rolling Message. The message is customizable using buttons. There are about 16 letter spaces available and you can edit the message to say whatever you want in the space available. The only disappointing thing is that once kill the power by removing the battery your message reverts to the pre-programed message.

One more thing that was disappointing but not a big deal. The kit was supposed to come with three button switches, but I was missing one. I was still able to build a functioning kit and use it, two of the buttons toggle through the alphabet up and down, so instead of having both buttons I only had an up button. The good news is that I sent an email to Velleman customer service and they promptly mailed me two more buttons, so I have a spare now.

In the end I highly recommend this kit for anyone. If you are looking for your first kit I would go with the 'Elenco AmeriKit Learn to Solder Kit' just because it comes with a basic soldering iron and tools, but the Rolling Message Display Kit is a good option for anyone else. And it's cool because the message is customizable. I changed the message to say 'Merry X-mas' and put it on top of our Christmas tree (you can use a battery to power the kit, but it also comes with a 9v AC plug you can add).

The Millenium Falcon was always my favorite Star Wars ship.  As soon as my son was old enough, I bought this for him. It is meant to be a display piece, but with the exception of losing the antenna dish (the cover on the cockpit is also removable, but not the cannons on top and bottom), its die-cast metal construction has stood the test of three years of little-boy play.  When he's too old for it, I may take it back and make it a display piece again!  Four stars instead of five due to the pieces that come off.
Arrived in perfect condition, and is Awesome!!!

When 80% of your product is plastic, having a giant !Die-Cast Model! listed on the front of the box is kind of misleading, Hasbro. But that's a naming issue and I'm not docking points for it.

This model is gorgeous! There is intricate attention to detail here, from the greebles on the model all the way to the paint job. It stands on three pillars which seem to be permanently attached to the underside of the Falcon. These screw into the plastic base. The plastic clear cover for the model is the kind that requires constant cleaning as it shows any dust collected from the air. So I didn't bother keeping it.

You can detach the top portion of the cockpit. Inside you will find the foursome of humans who usually inhabit the Falcon: Leia, Luke, Han and Chewie. The front bottom front landing gear folds down. The dish and guns look and feel flimsy enough that I haven't given any effort in trying to move them.

If you're a woodworker at all, you could ditch the plastic base and create a custom base that would allow you to screw in the three pillars that hold the model up in an angle that would let you view the model best.

I would have one of these in every room I ever planned on going in to everywhere, if I had my way. I highly recommend this if you're a fan of the original Star Wars trilogy, or the Millennium Falcon, or just incredibly cool spaceships.

This tool will save on belt wear and bearing wear. It is very easy to used and fairly accurate. For those complaining about the scale stickers peeling off, I agree, they should be etched on the metal. However, the distance scale is one to one with a ruler scale so if the decal falls off, just measure from the end of the device to the o-ring. The force scale is a simple linear spring, f=kx.
