I've been a fan of LEGO since the 1990s. It was cool to see a set of vintage Lego Minifigures I could add to my collection. The one I especially wanted was the White Ninja Princess from the late 90s "Ninja" line.

That being said, I'm rather disappointed with this set.

These Minifigs feel much lighter than the average Minifig that come with construction sets. It feels like cheap plastic. I did a comparison with some recent "City" series Minifigs, and there's a huge difference in weight and quality of plastic. Additionally, the White Ninja Princess' face is actually different from the original (At least the one in my set). The eyes are really close together, so she looks cross-eyed. That's too bad, since its the one I really wanted. I wish I had saw the product in person, since I probably would have decided against it. I don't believe this set is really worth the $30+.

Of course, they still function in every way as normal Minifigs, so you can still play with them, switch out parts and so forth. If you don't mind the plastic quality and possible bad print on the faces, they'd still be a really cool addition to any LEGO collection.

This is NOT a board game, there is no board, It is just the cards and the die in a coffin shaped box; which it does say in the product description "includes a custom coffin-shaped box with tray that holds the trivia cards and custom die...", but most people will assume that a board being included is a given with a trivial pursuit game, so they would assume it didn't need to be noted.

I came today to write this review (I made my purchase 3 days ago by the way, and had 2 day shipping via prime) and now it is only 12.79. Its not a huge difference but I feel cheated by the "deal of the day" sale that I bought it during, and a little cheated by the size and contents of the package as well.

That being said if you are okay with the price of the item for just a pack of cards and a die, packaged in an admittedly cool box then the questions themselves are fun and interesting for fans of the movie. I personally probably would not have paid the price I did for this had the product info been correct on the items size.

I use a lot of 'art sleeves,' sleeves that have art of characters I like or scenes that I thought were cool. However, without something to protect them, the sleeves fray and I wanted to get something to help preserve them.

I went to my friends, who all recommended Yanoman, which I tried out. They were relatively inexpensive and arrived faster than expected. It feels like a stuff plastic, but still malleable so be careful, you can still accidentally fold cards when you apply enough force. However, I bought a few other sleeves to test them out as well, since more options are helpful

I would say do not bother with any other oversleeves, these offer probably the best amount of protection that I could think of outside of encasing the card in a glass case. I also like the clear faces, letting me see the art and the card itself.

The only downside, to me, is I cannot just buy a few hundred at a time, they come in packs of 50 each.

Every time I pull this game out, half a dozen people decide to buy it. It's that fun and that hilarious, if you're willing to get into it. Like most games, if you can weave a story out of your game play, and goad the other players into pushing their luck too far, then you'll have a riot of a time playing this game.

If you're on the fence about this game, and have an iOS device, then download it for free from the App Store. The game only allows you to play against a single AI, but that's more than enough to give you the experience of playing the game (and either become addicted, or realize that it's not for you). For $0.99 you can even upgrade the app to a "pass-the-device" game for up to 8 people.

So, why not five stars? Because at the core, this game is all about luck. I tend to prefer games with a substantial element of decision-making and skill. Games of Zombie Dice are so short that your decision making really has very little effect on the outcome of a game (unless you have no clue when to stop rolling). It's like playing the lottery. Winning has almost nothing to do with you, and is almost entirely blind luck. That being said, it's still hilariously funny to play if you have the right group of people who will use it as a chance to brazenly interact with one another with trash talk, acting like zombies, laughing at each other, laughing at themselves, etc..

I really don't like Zombies. As a theme, it's not my cup of tea. But I can't deny this is a great game to play while waiting at a restaurant. The dice are high quality and the game play is simple yet engaging. If you're a fan of Farkle or other press your luck style games, then this is a must have for your collection. For $10, this game is well worth the price. However I do fault Steve Jackson games for the cheap presentation of the game. The game was initially released with a cheap cardboard tube dice shaker. Instead of releasing an updated second edition of the game, the company milks their fans with a separate dice bag and now a separate plastic dice shaker (sold separately!). Really?

I also purchased both expansions to the game, however I've not played either. Like the base game, the expansion contain quality dice, but piss for presentation. The game is all about the dice and the dice are well made, but everything else about the presentation is lack luster. That seems to be typical of SJ games.

The instructions included in the game are printed and worded terrible. Better to learn the game from better written sources. As for the variations of this game, Buck Hunter(?), and Dino Hunter, I would recommend buying the original Zombie Dice. The original has the best mix of simplicity and strategy.

Overall I give this a 4 star rating. I want to say it's a solid 5 star for the press your luck dice gamer like myself. It's a great game and they're quality dice, but the minimal presentation of the game really brings it down in over all review.

I buy a lot of boardgames, card games and dice games. That being said, I don't get to play most of those games very often. Since my main gaming companions are my 8-year old sister, and my 10-year old brother it's hard to find the right games to capture their interest and attention.

Luckily Zombie Dice is one of the games that they do enjoy and it gets plenty of play in our house. The main draws are how easy it is to learn, and how fast games tend to be. I managed to both learn how to play myself, and relay the rules to my siblings in just a matter of minutes. Games last about 10-15 minutes when playing with 2 people, and the length of the game increases slightly for each additional player (which is no surprise).

It's also rather compact, which makes it a good choice for a travel-friendly game. I recently brought it along on our family vacation, where we played it whenever we needed to entertain ourselves for a few minutes between activities and whatnot.

Being a dice game, it is obviously based significantly on luck, which is fine for me and my family, but if you are averse to games that are mostly based on luck then you probably wouldn't enjoy it.

Only took about 45 seconds to get the rules. Game (to 13 brains) took 5 minutes. Game to 31 took about 15 minutes. A perfect game to play when you want to do something quick. Or, when you're waiting for more people to arrive (it's easy to join in a game late, although you probably won't win...you entering will not affect or effect the strategy of those already playing).

Played it for the first time today, and it's a game I'll definitely be buying.

Bonus for parents with kids at or below teen ages: Although there is a high degree of luck in this game (due to, well, the sole action of rolling dice), it is, at its heart, a math game. It's all statistics--will the benefit of doing another roll outweigh the possible risk of "crapping out" for that turn? Since that calculation will largely depend on which of the various dice you have in your hand before each roll, it's a perfect opportunity to use statistics and probability theory in real-life and fun situations.

This game entertained us for hours.  It's especially good when you're waiting at the doctors office.  It doesn't take that many 'I got shot in the head' cries before somebody comes over and asks what's going on.

Pro: This game is extremely easy to learn, very fast paced, and the more people you have the more fun it is. A very simple dice game that has the whole party excited with each roll.

Con: For more advanced gamers it lacks a little depth, but the expansion helps with that a bit and it’s just so darn fun that replaying is never an issue, especially at a party or holiday with the family.

Kids: I have a 7 year old and a 5 year old who play and love this game. They love plans vs. zombies so they love the zombie theme and love the idea of being zombies themselves. They got the rules very quickly and because it is a dice game, this is one of the few games our 2 year old can play with our guidance where the physical part of the game he does on his own. (just pick up 3 dice and roll). This helps when playing a game as a family that sometimes is too complicated for him. I would recommend it for a mature 5 year old and certainly for 6 and 7 year olds and older who like games.

Other: Because it is a dice game, my brother who has Cerebral Palsy and struggles with fine motor control loved this game, because it was so easy for him to do physically. (He won a bunch of the games.)

Overall: This game rocks overall, the only con I had was a slight one, and people who play it once or see it played usually get excited to continue to play it. This has become a family favorite, it is also a game we know we can break out at any time, with any crowd anywhere and have fun with. We had a ton of fun, yelling and cheering on Christmas day at my uncles house, it was a blast (shotgun that is  )

Eat 13 brains, and you win! Yum!

What a great game! I have an 8 year old who's just starting to get into horror stuff. She wanted this game because of the zombie on the front, but once we started playing the game, she fell in love with it. It's an easy game with simple rules that can be played quickly. You rack up brains (points) and if you get shot 3 times, you get none for that round. All other dice can be re-rolled. First person to score the right amount of points wins. I believe the instructions say first person to either 13 or 14 points, but my daughter and I have easily adapted this to a 30-50 point game when we were really in the mood to play.

I would recommend this to any parent with kids over the age of 5. It's a great counting tool. The zombie drawing on the front is as graphic as anything in the game gets. The dice have a picture of a brain, one of a bang, and one of footprints. You could probably cover the cup they come in (and let your child draw on the cup), and tell your kids the pictures of brains are cotton candy or something. My daughter and I called brains "Points," Shotgun blasts "BANGS," and the footprints "Runners." Let your child decide what the brain shape looks like if you feel that "brains" is too graphic of a term for them.

My daughter and I have had HOURS of fun with this time killer.

First off, apologies for the terrible title. Now, about the game. Steve Jackson is a game designer with a lot of fun games to his credit and Zombie Dice is definitely among the good ones. The object is simple, throw the dice, which represent humans, and try to get as many brains as you can. Each side and color (red, yellow and green, from hardest to easiest) have a different combination of brains (points), feet (throw again) and shotgun blasts (get 3 and no points for you!). You have to throw 3 dice to score points and can bank your points anytime before deciding to throw. The first to 13 points wins.

I played Zombie Dice with 6 friends to decide turn order in another board game and we had almost as much fun as with the other game itself! It's great for non-gamers because they get it and connects them to their inner gambler I think. Check it out for a quick, easy and fun game to get into.

I got this for my nine year old son figuring it would make for some good family gaming. The rules are simple to learn and have just a trace of strategy to keep it from being a game of pure luck. The theme is well done with the artwork on the instructions, dice, and packaging all excellent.

In fact it's not a bad game at all. However I am calling this a novelty in my review and the reason for the three stars is it has very little replay value. It's fun a few times and then, at least in our family, it wasn't picked up again. There are lots of simple games that are played over and over again, but not this one.

Steve Jackson Games makes many great games; I've been a customer for years. Zombie Dice though, just doesn't have enough replay value to be more than a one day novelty. Maybe buy it for your Halloween party or other one time event and it's fine for that, but this game won't be the centerpiece of a family game night like many classics might be.

Need to teach your children to count to 13? Want to teach your kids how odds work? Zombie Dice is a very simply game. Roll dice until you either hit 13, or 3 shots... blam!

I bought two of these kits, one for each of my boys. They were very excited, and had fun playing with the parts and building a few of the kits. After a half-day of fun, the parts went into a box where they stayed.

There are so many little problems with the execution of this playset that add up to an unsatisfying experience:

1. There are two kinds of pegs -- one snap-fit, and one pressure-fit -- and both are red. And the instructions are so small, it's nearly impossible to figure out which one is required for a particular step. Some better color coding would've solved this... or better yet, just stick to a single kind of peg.

2. The pegs are hard to put in the holes, especially for little hands, and nearly impossible to remove. A prying tool is included specifically for this purpose, but a better design would've removed the need for the tool.

3. The remote controls use these perfectly flat buttons with no travel, so there's no feeling that you're doing anything by pressing them. There are also no lights; the only feedback is a "laser beam" sound that is not particularly pleasant for parents.

4. The remote controls use infrared signals, which have two drawbacks. First, they require some line-of-sight (although you don't have to point them directly at the models). Second, two remotes can't be used at the same time in the same room; my kids were frustrated because each was causing the other's model to move.

5. My kids liked the fact that they could control three motors independently, but the motors are very weak and run slowly. For most of the models included in the instructions, the motors are not strong enough to steer nor move the vehicles at a satisfying speed. Again, the kids were frustrated.

6. All the plastic parts are somewhat flexible. This is a bad thing when you have meshing gears and moving parts, as the beams often bend and twist instead of working as a rigid structure or mechanism.

7. Most of the parts are not symmetrical, which means they can only go together in certain ways. Kids (and grownups) accustomed to Lego will be disappointed at the limited possibilities. (Think 'Capsella' from many years ago.)

8. The parts are not compatible with Lego or any other building toys I've ever seen, making this a completely closed system. Once you've tried out the handful of ideas in the book, there's not much else you can do with this toy.

9. The instructions are tiny and hard to follow (again, poor when compared with Lego), and the pieces are hard to get together. Littler kids will need a lot of help from a grownup; older kids will likely become bored quickly with the limitations of this set.

10. The models you can build with the included instructions seem intriguing at first glance, but none of them work particularly well, requiring modification from the engineer in the family. I wish a little more thought had gone into this toy before it went to market.

Ultimately, this kit is a fail. Two stars because my kids did have fun, albeit for only a short time. I'm probably going to try to repurpose the remotes to work with Lego (which gets daily play in our house); otherwise, they entire set will just gather dust on the shelf.

My kids started playing with it and it seemed to work fine. As soon as the third motor was plugged in the battery pack (which controls the motors) started smoking. I unplugged the motor and inspected the internals (they have a clear case so you can see the motor, gears, and wiring). It turns out that the solder joints on all three motors are exposed. On the one that smoked the control board, the solder joints are both touching the motor case (causing the short that smoked the controller). I looked at the other two (also exposed solder joints) and they luckily only had one joint making contact with the motor housing, the other joint was just millimeters away from making contact. I'm going to contact Thames and Kosmos directly first to see if they will replace it since it's their poorly made product. They are closed for the holidays and will not be open until after the New Year.

As noted by another reviewer, if I plugged in a "good" motor in the slot that smoked, it constantly runs (no more control over it).

I like the idea of educational toys, but what's the point when it only creates disappointment for my kids on Christmas.

We initially got about 80% of this set at a garage sale when my 3 year old spotted the robot vehicle on the front of the box. Fast forward 3 months, and he's putting together basic box-shaped vehicles and has a clear 'vision' of what he wants (i.e. he screams at me if I don't put things exactly where he wants it), but I'm his peon, slaving to make robot vehicles every few days.

Pros: This is an awesome set making incredibly cool toys. I make my engineer friends make vehicles for my kid, and the adults get super into it. It's fun to then play with this complex, gear-driven vehicle that you've made. This is an awesome toy to understanding basic engineering concepts and make things that go vroom. Basically I recommend this building set wholeheartedly even though there are a couple of cons.

Cons:
- The comments about the manual drawings being too small is right on the nose -- it's hard to see which hole to put an attachments into, details of the gears and how they should fit together, etc. It's been trial and error, assembling different components and then realizing that something had to be one hole lower, etc.
- The parts break pretty easily. The 3 and 5 hole pieces have very thin edges that crack and result in loose holes for the red attachments, and we bent a wheel connecting stick and a several of the longer pieces that were attached together without any space to pry the red attachment pieces out. I am a medium sized woman, so this is not because I have super human strength. PSA/good news: they have a replacements department just for this purpose. It's not that cheap, but I think worth replacing/stocking up on parts with them if you are getting serious about the set.

This is a nice educational kit. I gave it to my 10 year old daughter recently. It uses plastic parts and plastic gears and snap-on parts. No screws or bolts are used. This has the advantage of being simple to build but the disadvantage that machines do not work well. The cars are fine but things that use lots of gears and specially those that put stress on gears tend to fail. The Robotic Arm is a good example. The gears that lift the arm up and down too flimsy to handle the weight of the fully extended arms. I was initially disappointed but then realized this is a good opportunity to teach my daughter how things break. It demonstrates the principle of levers very well. Because the gears handle the arm when the hands are pulled in but fail when the hands are extended. If I reinforce the gears buy holding them tight with my hands I can show how to compensate for the additional strain. So while the arm is not practical as a robotic arm it is very practical as a tool to show how they work and where the stress points are.

I use this servo to control the arms of an animatronic skeleton I set up every Halloween.  I use to two of them at each shoulder joint, one to move the arm mechanism (which contains a lighter servo at the forearm joint ) frontward and backwards and the other to raise the arm upwards and downwards.  While the resulting effect is very lifelike, it exposes the shoulder servos to quite a bit of stress, given they are in essence connected to the end of a relatively large "boom" consisting of the arms, especially when they are engaged in rapid movements. I initially found that the latter caused the round plastic servo horns these come with to crack, so I replaced those with round diecast metal ones that are attached to the arm connections by tightly fastened hex bolts.  The only improvement I wish hitec could make to these servos would be to redesign the servo hub to accept a beefier screw to secure the horn.. The ones it currently  employs are thin 2.6mm ones, which are much too light given the stresses this servo is exposed to in robotic/animatronic applications.

These things never really break... they simply pop off during a crash and are never to be found again. Who wants to miss out on flying because a silly head link popped off and disappeared? Not me, and not you either! When I take the Blade 120 SR out to fly, I keep a little baggy of spare head links and grommets in the box that holds the spare batteries.

When putting these links on the heli, make sure the little casting mark (small raised dot at the center of the link) is pointed out, away from the shaft. That will put the head link in the correct position, which means the larger side of the link's holes are facing the shaft, and the smaller sides are on the outside of the ball links. This makes a HUGE difference in flying, as the other way around will stiffen the overall head movement (causing TBE) and the links will be more apt to get pushed past the ball onto the arms, which really causes poor handling!

Make these one of your essential crash kit items.

This worked great.  It sprouted crystals in under an hour and was completely finished in a day's time.  Left undisturbed, it has sat and looked beautiful for a month now.  But it is finally starting to dry out and the "leaves" are starting to fall off little by little.  Last night I got the bright idea that maybe it needed some more water to maintain its leaves, so I poured a little water in the tray.  Well that wasn't a good idea because while it soaked up the water, the fragile crystal leaves apparently couldn't hold any moisture in their delicate state, so this morning 20% of the tree has fallen off.  One other suggestion... you will need to separate the paper branches before you start growing this.  I separated them but did not actually fold and bend them to stay in that position.  As a result, when the water started to wick up, many of the branches went back to their original position--right alongside the other branches and we didn't get the separation of the branches as much as we could have.  Final comment:  I only gave this 4 stars because I found this exact product at Hobby Lobby for $2.99 so I feel like it is overpriced here--especially as an add-on item.

Ascension is a deck building game for 2-4 players. You will start with 10 cards in your deck and purchase more cards from the center table as the game goes on. By building your deck up this way (I tend to end games around 40-60 cards in my deck) you earn honor points, which are added up at the end of the game to determine a winner. Deck-building is not a new concept, but it isn't tired either. The competitive nature of Ascension deals in the board in the center. All players are building decks at the same time while taking turns around the table. Ascension has 6 cards available for purchase in the center at a time. This means that if you plan to buy a card from the middle, the person on your right might buy it before you, leaving you with a less or more exciting market when it is your turn. I rather enjoy Ascension, but the communal market of single cards is the thing that makes it harder for me to play.

Pros:

Ascension is easy to explain, and the board has a turn walkthrough on it so you can't get lost on your first play of the game.

There are a lot of cards that do a lot of neat things, and all cards are in every game.

----------------------------------------------
Cons:

I find the shared market extremely frustrating. I prefer skill to luck in my games, while this does not eliminate skill, it pushes luck more than other deck-building games.

As an easy to learn game, it uses two currencies during play. I understand why both are there, but it can become difficult for some people to remember what they have of each currency. Note: neither of the two currencies mentioned are the Honor Points, which you need to win the game. It can get confusing.

---------------------------------------------------

I have enjoyed Ascension in the past, but I have grown out of it recently. I prefer Dominion for the tactics and strategy there, where I find Ascension annoying while other players are going their turn. In other games I plan my turns ahead of time, but Ascension's market changes each turn, so if you plan ahead you will just upset yourself in the end.

There is an iOS app for iPhone and iPad for Ascension. It is very good and you can pass around one phone to play it with a group or in a car ride or something. It is a good cheap way to see what you think of the game, but remember, if you are passing and playing the app, you are not seeing the frustration of watching that market change constantly while you hold your next turn's hand.

Ascension is a deck-building game, which allows players to "buy" cards using in-game resources, and assemble a deck that is used to win the game. For those already familiar with games like Dominion, then you will encounter a game that is very much the same. For those not familiar with Dominion, I think this serves as a good gateway to those kind of games. It's very quick-paced and fun, with the average game a little under a half hour in length, serving both casual players and more strategically minded players very well. It's also accessable to ages 10 and up, though some of the artwork might not be the best for some kids. (So, parents, do keep that in mind.) But, as a game for learning how to achieve a goal and problem solving, it's a very excellent game to play in the family. It teaches critical thinking skills (in terms of determining and assembling the tools you need to win), promoting a bit of healthy competition, without getting too long and tedious as with some games. Further, while the ultimate goal is the same (to achieve a certain score), there's many paths to achieving that goal.

Overall, an enjoyable game that is good for 3 to 4 people for about a half hour of play.

I have played all the big deck building games and Ascension is my favorite followed by Dominion.

One way to look at Ascension is as an evolution of magic without the mechanics and core elements that revolve around buying new cards. At its core Magic is flawed because it has such a strong business model that is based on players buying more cards that is sways the nature of the game and the balance of decks. In Ascension your deck is limited and is the same deck for both players (although a massive deck at that ). It also doesn't have the friendship breaking, hard core, player vs player mechanics (a negative for some), but instead is a bit more passive in its player interaction (although there are plenty of cards that are not). It's more akin to playing a game of cards (with a traditional card deck) in that you have to keep a very close eye on the other players choices in order to best work out your strategy.

At first play, it's tempting to say that the game involves a lot of luck, but there is much less luck than a game of Magic (one that boasts hard core tournaments). It's taken me about 12 games to begin to see the real depth of this game. There's a component of randomness to the game, in that you don't know what the deck will reveal, but good players can either adjust or manage their hands in a way to accommodate. The randomness affects both players. Plus, a bit of randomness for me means you aren't sweating out 4-5 moves down the line and getting all decision fatigued like in a game of chess.

Also, the art isn't my favorite, but it has grown on me and I think is improved in the expansion. The theme is okay but I do feel like the cards themselves have personality and that the mix of each is well balanced and has a great deal of depth in learning which ones to focus on.

Given this small complaints, the core mechanics coupled with the speed of the game makes the gameplay outstanding. It plays best with two players and diminishes a bit in fun as you add more. More players slow the speed of the game up, and although still a lot of fun, it's not as good as the 2 player game.

Who is this game not for, well if you aren't into hand management games, or want something with a more aggressive Player vs Player option, then it's not for you, but then, I don't think any deck-building game would be. It's also not for you if you like the meatier, more thematic Eurogames. This game isn't light, I would say it's streamlined. it's fast, addictive, and even if you lose, you get a lot of enjoyment in just building a deck with your favorite cards.

I prefer this over Dominion because although a great game, this game has more interaction with the player. I prefer it over Thunderstone because I find Thunderstone to be slower and less accessible, I also think this game might have more strategic depth but I am sure there are players who could disprove me.

If you are on the fence and have an iPhone, pickup the iPhone game for $5, a great deal, and sample the gameplay. Like with all good games, give it about 5 games and see what you think.

This has such high reviews on Amazon that I feel like I'm missing something.

Full disclosure, I've only played one deck building game before this one and that was Dominion. In addition, I only played one game.

I've had this game sitting on the shelf for a while and finally decided to give it a whirl. Since then I've played four games. Three of them consisted of two players and the other one was three players.

The first game, which was a two player game, was our first time so we were learning the game as we went along. We had no idea if the decks what we were building were good or bad. Eventually when all the honor was gone, we tallied up our points. I was shocked to find out that the winner had double the number of points than the other person.

This gave me an uneasy feeling that the game was unbalanced.

We started the second game (also just a two player game) with a little more experience under our belt. After a few rounds of play, it seemed like one player was going to runaway with the game leaving the other player in the dust. I was right; no contest.

The third game was played with three players. It was not apparent that there was a clear winner in the beginning. However, once the game ended the winner had nearly as many points as the other two players combined.

The fourth game was largely the same as the second one that I played. Within a few rounds I knew that the game was essentially over. When the points were tallied, my suspicions were confirmed. Another runaway leader.

In the two player games where I suspected that the game might essentially be over in a few rounds, it wasn't due to some dazzling display of strategy. Just dumb luck, plain and simple. A few random card draws allowed one player to build their deck with what seemed like exponential power while the other one was building on a linear scale.

To me a game is fun, when there is a healthy dose of competition. A game in which one person takes off from the beginning and can't be challenged isn't fun (even if you're the one winning).

I'm left wondering of the expansion packs even this out or if the game tends to create a runaway leader more often than not.

At ~$20 to ~$30 a piece, I'm not sure if I'm willing to find out.

Like the similar [[ASIN:1594721270 Thunderstone]], Ascension is a fantasy-themed knock-off of Dominion but with a bit of steampunk thrown in. Like Thunderstone, Ascension is okay: playable but I didn't have much fun. It is faster to set-up and play, as well as easier to learn, than Thunderstone. Each player gets a starter deck of eight Apprentice (money) and 2 Militia (attack) cards. You draw 5 cards into your hand each turn and can use the money and attack points to buy more heroes or items and to kill monsters. Monsters you defeat grant victory points and may also provide other bonuses.

Ascension comes with a plastic storage tray, crappy quality cards and surprisingly, a game board that shows you where to place all the cards. One bizarre, totally unnecessary but kind of neat extra is it comes with a bunch of small, hard plastic uncut diamonds (1 victory point markers) and larger rubies (5 victory point markers); the typical punch-out cardboard tokens would have been more practical though, and might have brought the game's cost down. The rulebook is badly formatted; we couldn't easily find a rule we had a question about despite multiple people reading through the book looking for it in our first game! Finally, although not everyone in my group had a problem with it, Ascension loses major points with me for its amateur, weirdly stylized artwork. I didn't like looking at any of the card art so that impacted my enjoyment of the game as well.

To be fair, in some ways I do like Ascension better than Thunderstone (faster set-up, easier to learn, and you are only limited in your actions by your money and attack power so you can buy or kill multiple things each turn, a major advantage over Thunderstone). However, even with Ascension's streamlining, the two games are so darn similar in mechanics that they are practically the same except Thunderstone has better art, already has an expansion set and is fully customizable to suit your fancy. But for all of Thunderstone's neat deck customization options, I'm not sure the extra trouble is worth it... It takes too long to sort the various decks (and then decide which cards to play with, not to mention the extra time each player's turn can take while trying to pick from so many options). In the end, neither game was very much fun for me so I can't really recommend Ascension or Thunderstone. I wouldn't play either game again. I do, however, strongly recommend [[ASIN:B001JQY6K4 Dominion]] and its expansions if you're looking for a fun, customizable deck-building card game with a medieval/Renaissance flavor.

A Disclosure: I did not buy this game, but have played it many times with friends at games stores, for what would equate to about 4 hours of total play time. The dominant strategies involve trying to discard/remove lower value cards from your deck while quickly gathering as much of the fixed supply of point tokens and more powerful center cards, with the winner gathering the most total point tokens + total value of deck cards. The first few times through it's fairly interesting.

Then you realize that you're essentially playing variety of solitaire in competition with the opposing player.

There is very little room for any real strategy, and much of the outcome of the game is based on luck-of-the-draw. Competition, such as it is, exists in who is able to buy/kill the cards in the communal/central hand first and to greater effect. The winner can usually be determined after only a few rounds, and after a few short games game-play can feel mechanical and rote.

I recently Ascension: Chronicle of the Godslayer by GaryGames. Ascension is a deckbuilding game. What does that mean? For those of you familiar with Dominion, this is a game using the same basic mechanic. For those of you new to the concept, here's how it goes. You start the game with a deck. A small deck. On your turn, you acquire cards for your deck. Purchased cards typically go into your discard pile, which you shuffle when you get through your deck. This adds purchasing power for better cards and easier acquisition of point cards. You shuffle a lot. The concept started with Dominion, and has been popping up in other games like Thunderstone and Ascension. It's a good mechanic, and it makes games that use it easy to teach since you begin the game with a premade deck of just a couple different kinds of cards.

Ascension works for 2-4 players. It has a board to organize the cards. There is a deck of characters and monsters to defeat or purchase, and some spiffy plastic crystals to track Honor (victory points). Six cards are laid out on the board, and these are either going to be characters which go in your deck (purchased with Runes), monsters to defeat using Power) or constructs, which are cards that go through your deck that you may play into your play area and they stay in effect indefinitely. Most characters will provide Power and/or Runes. Runes are the currency you use to buy cards, and Power is what you use to defeat enemies. You can buy as many characters or constructs and/or fight as many enemies as you have the Power and Runes for in your turn.

I like this game quite a bit. It is simple. Ascension is much less of an endeavor to set up than Dominion is. We own all the released sets for Dominion, and it really seems like a monumental undertaking to set up. Ascension, on the other hand, just requires you to grab a starting deck, lay out the board, shuffle the card supply and count out the Honor crystals. The simplicity of this makes it much more likely to hit the table if just my wife and I are playing. Additionally, the possibility of buying characters and fighting enemies in the same turn is nice, especially compared to Thunderstone's clunky dungeon mechanic. Definitely worth it!

The first in the Gryphon Games Family Games series is Charon Inc., which is a reference to one of the moons of the planet (?) Pluto. In the game, players assume the role of CEOs (here's hoping that the corner office and six-figure salary will be included in a later expansion) of a space-age mining corporations that are leading the rush to colonize and mine the moon Charon. As a player, your job is to lay claim to the various mining regions of the moon and, utilizing the resources you collect, construct various buildings that will ultimately earn you victory points at the conclusion of the game.

The components are quite nice, and there's some interesting area control mechanics, combined with a choice of special abilities players can compete for each round. The heart of the game is about placing flags to stake your claim in areas on Charon, which in turn will get you the gems (minerals) needed to complete various point-scoring buildings - represented in the game by cards. The theme is arguably pasted on, but using flags for the claim-staking area control mechanic does give the game a certain charm.

Charon Inc is not something for the AP-prone, and won't suit the tastes of every group, but there are certainly going to be gamers who really enjoy this style of game. Also be aware that the game will feel quite different depending on the number of players (2-5). - EndersGame @ BGG

This is a sealed MTG booster box. If you haven't purchased one before, they consist of 36x booster packs, with 15 cards per pack. Your draw is relatively random. The Scars of Mirrodin, Mirrodin Besieged, and New Phyrexia boxes contain a ton of artifact creatures, artifacts and artifact equipment. This is a fun set with poison counters and futuristic artwork.

The latest (as of this review) expansion to the Magic: The Gathering line is the Scars of Mirrodin. It is an artifact-heavy block, so expect 50% or greater of the cards you open to be colorless artifacts. I have so far only played with boosters from then prerelease, so my experience is somewhat limited. So far, I am not especially impressed. Decks built from this block will, unexpectedly, be loaded with colorless artifacts, especially if you are doing a limited format. After a few playthroughs with a limited deck that I made from these boosters, I walked away not feeling like this set really did anything new and innovative. That being said, I expect that when I combine with my 2011 cards I expect I will be happy with the results, but on a stand-alone basis, I cannot recommend it too strongly.

Recently got back into Legoes, so I filled up my cart with lots of different kits, including this one. It came on time and the box arrived undamaged.

I noticed a tiny piece of plastic chipped off of a brick, but I paid it no mind as I wanted to get building. Assembly was normal, except for a strange clicking sound. I've built quite a few kits by now, and that was the first time I've heard it. Once I got to the roof assembly, I immediately saw why: the pieces were brittle. They cracked as I snapped them in place. Maybe a third or so of the roof bricks are broken just from typical use exactly once. Same thing happened with the trunk of the tree. Bit bummed out by that.

I'd knock off a star or two for that, but this is an old set. I saw 2011 on the back of the instructions and, as I said, I saw no evidence that this was anyone's fault. It's not like the box was crunched or I got a used set or anything.

The set strikes a nice balance between building a large structure with basic bricks and having a few special pieces that add some flair. It's also a great demonstration of how the same pieces can be used different ways. (I especially like how the bowed pieces are used for the frame of a canoe in one build and an awning in another.)

Overall I'd recommend this as a great starter set for any child who's graduating out of Duplo into "big kid" Legos.

screw holes didn't line up and fell apart within a month of use.  (just after return period).  Great concept as my boys 2 and 5 loved playing with it but the cheap craftsmanship made this a true disappointment.  I now have wall pieces laying around my living room.
Peels apart

This play set was very cute, but terrible quality. There were many dents and scratches, all of the corners were messed up, and the assembly instructions were terrible. Had this been a $20 or $30 toy I would not have been so disappointed, but for almost $90 I would expect more.
Hard to assemble.

I don't mind this play house. But i have had it for just a few days over a month and my daughter has maybe played with it 6 times and there is already pieces breaking. This comes with pre-drilled holes to screw into but i found the holes were too big... So i have pieces coming apart because the screws will not stay in the pre-drilled holes. Additional the fire and police guys clothes (paint color) are peeling off. I don't think the quality is there for the price.

Other than the few issues I had with broken pieces, the product is pretty decent. I am disappointed it wasn't in better condition considering it wasn't exactly a cheap item. One of the boards/walls is completely cracked down the middle. The helicopter was broken. The part that connects the playset shut wouldn't stay on as the screws were both stripped. It took a lot of super glue to get the thing together and with it being a gift that I have to give very soon I didn't have time to return or believe me, i would have. Several of the walls are also peeling as if they had gotten wet as well. Not super impressed but now that it is together I am hoping it stays together and will still be enjoyed.
This wasn't played with by my son, and its huge. Wasn't very impressed.

We have an 18 month old who spends some time each day with this toy. He especially loves some of the furniture pieces that he carries around the apartment as well as opening and closing the doors. We put away a number of the items that came with it as they are too small for hims still (I think the recommended age on this toy is 3 years).

Cons - installation & quality - the item comes partially installed in that there are two parts that you need to screw together using 2 hinges that each come with screws. Unfortunately, the screws are flat and there are no existing holes. I guess you need a drill. We are very low tech, so we had to use a hammer, which wasn't great. Then one of the hinges that came already attached as part of the other side of the house was missing the piece that keeps it together. I had to email the manufacturer since they don't answer their phone. They did email me back within 1-2 days and they did mail me a replacement for this part, which we then installed by unscrewing the defective hinge. Had I known we would be doing this much assembly, I might have looked for a fully assembled item. Also, the house feels a little flimsy.
Brother and Sister say my nephew loves it.

First let me say my grandson love this. It is actually a nice little play set. It will take about an hour to assemble and at times I had to take back apart what I put together and flip parts around because the instructions are pretty generic. That being said if you can deal with that it is pretty good

I ordered this for my 10yo grandson. The assembly instructions are seriously lacking. I'm very good at assembly and I had to take it apart multiple times. Tip: pay attention to the screw heads, they are colored to match various boards. One board was cracked upon arrival, but otherwise it seemed very sturdy.
All that said, my grandson loved it and I'm "the best!".😉

I purchased this on Amazon right after Thanksgiving for an incredible price and Prime shipping.  I compared this version with a smaller version and am happy I went with this larger version. The entire firehouse is made of wood. And one of the best features is the entire firehouse opens up for play and then shuts for clean up. We gave this to our 4 year old son last year for Christmas and he loves it. It came with great accessories such as furniture, vehicles, dogs, dog bowls and 2 bendable firemen.  I purchased additional figures for him to use with it. The helicopter, motorcycle and firetruck are all made of wood. My only complaints are the ladders on the sides of the firetruck do not stay on, the toy basketball goal is too light and falls over and a screw keeps coming out of one of the doors which leaves the door hanging. But it truly is a great toy and it encourages imaginative play.

My 5 year old is EXTREMELY destructive. (As in he can disassemble his entire bed frame - and has.) But this toy has made it 2 months already. It is made of laminated chipboard so I assumed it would break easily but so far it has held up well despite my boys using it as a jumping stool. We do try to keep them from doing this as much as possible as I imagine that it will eventually break but thus far, its help up well.

I did have to take away 1 star because
A.) it was very time consuming to put together and
B.) there was one section that did not fit together exactly right and ended up needing a quick fix from my hubby.

Overall - great product and I would definitely recommend it!

My kids love this play set and a year later it is still one of their favorite toys.  The actual building has held up well to constant play by a bunch of small children.  The firetruck and helicopter have not fared so well.  The blades on the helicopter were only glued in and came apart the first time my kids played with it.  The back part of the firetruck came un-glued as well after a few weeks of play.

We bought this for my son's 3rd birthday. He absolutely loves it and it was super simple to put together. We love Kidkraft products and this one certainly didn't disappoint! The only reason why I gave it 4 stars instead of 5 is because i find the stairs at the top near the helipad and the 3 yellow 'gates' surrounding the basketball area flimsy and they often fall off when playing or closing/opening the unit. Overall a great set though!

We were deciding between this set and the KidKraft Deluxe Fire Rescue (available on manufacturer site). Our 2 year old definitively told Santa "that one, that one". But alas the Everyday Heroes set does not come with an ambulance, which Santa did not know was such a big deal. He actually cried on Christmas and now asks hopefully every time he gets a present, "Is that the ambulance?" The problem is that you can only buy the ambulance on the Kidkraft site as a part of a set (with a firetruck that we already have at $30 +$15 shipping) and it takes 10 days to come. As both his parents are medical professionals as well, we are also sad that the ambulance is not part of the "heroes" set. (It does by the way come with a motor cycle which was appreciated, but apparently did not make up for the lack of an ambulance.)

We otherwise would have given the product 5 stars. It takes about an hour to put together but it is solid, the doors function well and the furniture pieces are well-done. Great "doll-house" alternative for boys.
A little difficult to assemble, but I’m not very mechanical. My 5 yo grandson loves playing with this.
I could not have chosen a better gift for my 3 year old grandson. He plays with it daily bringing his other toys such as cars and figures along and lets his imagination fly!

I want to give this playhouse 5 stars bc its a fabulous playhouse for Toddlers but you need to know its not in perfect condition. Its durable and it will be awesome for my lil ones but it has some paint smudges and it has a couple knicks. It was packaged very very well, so the knicks happened at the factory. The other thing that is annoying is the plastic door doesn't stay shut. I may just stick some double sided tape in the doorway.
The furniture and heros are fabulously adorable! Super easy assembly. I also love how it closes up to 1/2 its size and you can store the accessories inside.
Again, it will be awesome for the kids and Im excited to give it to them!

It was a little tough to put together. This toy keeps both my kids (5 & 7) busy for hours. HOURS! Together and not fighting! I'd almost want to say it's a little miracle in this big box.
In all seriousness there is enough room for the kids to each play without getting in each other's way.

It was the perfect size for my toddler he’s turning two and I got this for him as a gift. He’s played with it everyday and enjoys the little pieces of furniture. Very well made product and cool little fort for his action figures.

My 3 year old son received this as a birthday present and LOVED it! He really likes the vehicles - fire engine, motorcycle, and helicopter - and the fact that the people bend to ride them. I like that it folds up easily and everything fits inside. It did take me a while to put it together, but instructions were fairly helpful. Pieces were all numbered, nothing was missing. I would recommend for young kids, boys and girls (we have girl cousins and friends that also love it). Maybe not for homes with babies learning to walk. Small furniture pieces have rounded edges, but actual building has sharp outside corners.

My grandson wanted a house like his sister has and this was perfect. The best hero house ever! Now my granddaughter wants one like this too. We love that it folds shut for easy clean up. Everyone that saw this would like to have had one or wants to get one. Little boys need a doll (hero) house too.

My little brother wanted this so much. When I looked it up I didn’t think it would amount to much amusement, but when we actually got it, it proved to be a fun way to spend time with the family. Great game, good bonding moment!
My son enjoys his new toy play set, it was easy to assemble and so many accessories came with the house. 5 stars for sure

My son was constantly playing with his sister's doll house.  This is an awesome product for boys who want a "house" for their action figures to play in.  The instructions for assembly are not easy to follow, but doable with a few attempts.  Great product and kidkraft has not let us down.
SENSATIONAL!!!! this is HUGE and amazing, took me over an hour to build but that's only because it's so amazing with so many cool things! People who lost screws... That's your fault lol
Sturdy, good construction.  Gets used everyday, lots of interesting pieces.  Well designed and it folds up for storage.

Super sturdy. My 2.5 year old loves it! The only assembly required was screwing screws into a set of hinges and the screw holes weren't drilled large enough... had to muscle them in. Also, part of the laminate flooring in one of the rooms was coming up and damaged. Had to tape it down. Aside from that, I'd say it's very good quality. Solid as a rock. I don't think it comes with enough people and furniture. I guess that's how they get you to buy more. Anyhow, I still gave it 5 stars. It's not often you find nice wooden toys that hold a toddler's attention for long. This one does!
The best investment you can possibly make for a child who is OBSESSED with firefighters! My son is so so so in love with this toy! It is a great alternative to a doll house!

This is a very well made toy. It is a little smaller then what I had expected, but my son loves it. He and my daughter both have played with it non stop since they got it. We have bought several products from Kid Kraft and have never been disappointed. This toy comes mostly put together so its easy to set up.

We got this play set for my  3 1/2 yr old son for Christmas and he loves it! The set itself is pretty large but I love that the two sides are on hinges and can close to condense it. There are a ton of pieces to use in the playset that are all made of wood and easy to identify their purpose. The pieces are durable for the most part with the exception of a few like the treadmill that has black mesh on it and will probably rip over time. Not a big deal in my world. Great play set! Glad that things like this exist for boys too!
[[VIDEOID:90b63eaf5b2ec5073bdf02c0e26ad4e3]] My 3 year old grandson loves this, and so does his 1 1/2  sister. Very easy to put together and very sturdy!
[[VIDEOID:1035ca1b115b393c9fef23e69e8232b3]] Seems good quality. Easy to assemble..

I bought this as a Christmas present for my 2-year old son, and he hasn't stopped playing with it since. I was going back and forth with buying a train table or this set, and I decided to go with this, the biggest plus being its a huge space saver, and it's more portable to move from living room to upstairs playroom. The pictures show how it looks closed, versus all compartments opened up. I'm a female and I was able to put this together without any difficulty, instructions were clear, although my husband finished building the whole set. Don't hesitate to buy, 100% satisfied!

The best gift I’ve ever given my grandson!  At 5 yrs of age he has lots of figures and characters but seemed a little sad that he didn’t have a house for them like his sister’s princess castles and Barbie houses.  The wooden material is so much sturdier than plastjc would be and is very well painted with stationary decorations (much better than peel off stickers like you’d get on most models)!  It can be adapted to any kind of police/emergency, super heroes and villains , animal figures and other collectible characters.  It is spacious, the furnishings are adorable, and the best part is that it folds up to keep all the small pieces stored, taking up much less space than when it’s opened up.  The only negative about this is the difficulty of assembly.  Come on, Kidcraft, how hard would it be to include some actual instructions?!*#?!

After putting this together for my 5 year old bday the screws starting falling out and makes ng it fall apart the first day!!! Not what I would expect for this price!!! I had to get my own screws and try and rebuild... as their acres fall out I am trying to replace with other screw that I find that work better?!?! Very disappointed!
Thought it was a great price, did not hold up well at all, even before much use. Walls fall apart very easily.
